1	"""Spawn a streaming ``claude -p`` worker for one issue and record its usage.
2	
3	The orchestrator owns the cycle loop and the Linear lifecycle; this module
4	owns one spawned session: how it is launched, how its token usage is
5	parsed off the wire, and how it is force-terminated when it overruns the
6	per-issue time cap.
7	
8	**Why stream-json.** A bare ``claude -p`` prints only the final assistant
9	text; the run log then records exit code and wall-clock and nothing about
10	spend. ``--output-format stream-json`` (requires ``--verbose``) emits one
11	JSON event per line — per-turn ``assistant`` messages plus a terminal
12	``result`` — from which we reconstruct exactly what the session cost.
13	
14	**Token accounting.** Each ``assistant`` event carries ``message.usage``,
15	but the same assistant message is emitted *once per content block*
16	(thinking, text, tool_use) — every copy repeats the identical usage under
17	the identical ``message.id``. Summing per event therefore double- (or
18	triple-) counts a turn; we key by ``message.id`` so each turn's usage is
19	counted exactly once. ``cumulative`` is the sum across turns of all four
20	token components — the real billed-token figure, dominated in long
21	tool-use loops by cache reads re-paid every turn. ``peak_context`` is the
22	largest single-turn context (input + cache_read + cache_create), i.e. how
23	close the session came to the context-window ceiling.
24	
25	The terminal ``result`` event is authoritative for ``cost_usd``
26	(``total_cost_usd``), ``num_turns``, ``session_id`` and ``is_error``. Note
27	``result.usage`` is only the *final* turn's snapshot, not the session
28	total, so it is deliberately not used for the token figures — those come
29	from the accumulated per-turn stream, which is also what survives a kill
30	before any ``result`` arrives.
31	
32	**Guardrails — belt and suspenders.** ``per_issue_cost_usd`` is handed to
33	``claude`` as ``--max-budget-usd`` so the session self-terminates on cost
34	(the native belt). The token and time caps are the orchestrator's
35	suspenders: a poll loop watches the live cumulative-token tally and the
36	elapsed wall-clock, and SIGKILLs the session the moment either is crossed.
37	Any cap passed as ``None`` is not enforced; with both token and time caps
38	off the worker simply waits for the session to exit on its own. A crossed
39	cap is reported back as a ``Breach`` so the orchestrator can name it in the
40	halt entry. See ``limits.py`` for the thresholds and their precedence.
41	
42	**Process-group kill.** The worker is launched with
43	``start_new_session=True`` so it leads its own process group. On a breach
44	the whole group is SIGKILLed via ``os.killpg``, reaping grandchildren —
45	MCP servers, sub-agents — that a kill of the direct child alone would
46	orphan to keep burning tokens. SIGKILL (not a SIGTERM grace) is used
47	deliberately: a session being force-terminated past a guardrail has no
48	clean-shutdown work worth waiting for, and SIGKILL is the only signal a
49	misbehaving grandchild cannot ignore. Killing the whole group also closes
50	the stdout pipe those grandchildren inherited, which is what lets the
51	reader thread reach EOF instead of blocking forever.
52	"""
53	from __future__ import annotations
54	
55	import json
56	import os
57	import signal
58	import subprocess
59	import sys
60	import threading
61	import time
62	from dataclasses import dataclass
63	from pathlib import Path
64	from typing import Any, Callable, TextIO
65	
66	from .limits import Breach
67	
68	_STREAM_FLAGS = ["--verbose", "--output-format", "stream-json"]
69	"""Flags that switch ``claude -p`` into line-delimited JSON event mode.
70	``--verbose`` is required: without it ``stream-json`` emits only the
71	terminal ``result`` event, dropping the per-turn ``assistant`` usage we
72	accumulate."""
73	
74	_READER_JOIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 5.0
75	"""Bound on waiting for the stdout-reader thread to drain to EOF after the
76	process exits. The group SIGKILL closes every inherited write-end of the
77	pipe, so EOF normally arrives at once; the bound only guards the
78	pathological case of a group member that somehow escaped the kill, so the
79	worker returns with the usage it has rather than hanging."""
80	
81	_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 1.0
82	"""How often the breach monitor wakes to compare the live token tally and
83	elapsed wall-clock against the per-issue caps. A worry-free overshoot of at
84	most one interval before the kill lands; one second is responsive enough for
85	a session measured in minutes and cheap enough to poll."""
86	
87	_TOKEN_FIELDS = (
88	    "input_tokens",
89	    "output_tokens",
90	    "cache_creation_input_tokens",
91	    "cache_read_input_tokens",
92	)
93	
94	
95	@dataclass
96	class WorkerResult:
97	    """Outcome of one spawned session, recorded into the run-log entry.
98	
99	    ``exit_code`` is the process return code (negative when killed by a
100	    signal, e.g. ``-9`` on a guardrail SIGKILL). ``breach`` is the branch
101	    selector the orchestrator reads: ``None`` when the session ran to its
102	    own exit, otherwise the per-issue token or time cap that was crossed,
103	    carrying the cap and the value observed at kill time. ``usage`` carries
104	    the four token components plus ``cumulative`` and ``peak_context``.
105	    ``cost_usd`` / ``num_turns`` / ``session_id`` / ``is_error`` come from
106	    the terminal ``result`` event and are ``None`` when the session was
107	    killed before one arrived.
108	    """
109	
110	    exit_code: int
111	    breach: Breach | None
112	    duration_seconds: float
113	    model: str
114	    usage: dict[str, int]
115	    cost_usd: float | None
116	    num_turns: int | None
117	    session_id: str | None
118	    is_error: bool | None
119	
120	
121	def run_issue(
122	    *,
123	    claude_cmd: list[str],
124	    model: str,
125	    prompt: str,
126	    cwd: Path,
127	    token_limit: int | None,
128	    time_limit_seconds: float | None,
129	    cost_limit_usd: float | None,
130	    passthrough: TextIO | None = None,
131	    debug_file: Path | None = None,
132	    poll_interval_seconds: float = _POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS,
133	    on_progress: Callable[[int, int, int, float | None, float], None] | None = None,
134	) -> WorkerResult:
135	    """Spawn one streaming ``claude -p`` session and return its usage.
136	
137	    ``claude_cmd`` is the base argv (``["claude", "-p",
138	    "--dangerously-skip-permissions"]``); the streaming flags, ``--model``,
139	    ``--max-budget-usd`` (when ``cost_limit_usd`` is set), and the prompt are
140	    appended here. The session runs in its own process group; if its
141	    cumulative tokens cross ``token_limit`` or its wall-clock crosses
142	    ``time_limit_seconds`` the whole group is killed and the returned
143	    ``WorkerResult.breach`` names the cap. A limit passed as ``None`` is not
144	    enforced. ``cost_limit_usd`` is enforced by ``claude`` itself, not here.
145	
146	    Non-JSON lines on the merged stdout/stderr stream (claude warnings,
147	    hook stderr) are written to ``passthrough`` (default ``sys.stderr``) so
148	    the operator still sees diagnostics; recognised JSON events are
149	    consumed by the usage parser.
150	
151	    ``debug_file``, when set, is passed to ``claude`` as ``--debug-file`` so
152	    the session writes its startup diagnostics — which settings sources,
153	    plugins, MCP servers, and hooks initialised — to that path. Debug logs
154	    go to the file, not stderr, so the merged stream the usage parser reads
155	    is unaffected. Opt-in only; ``None`` (the default) passes no flag and the
156	    session behaves exactly as before. See ``docs/design-decisions.md`` §10.
157	
158	    ``on_progress``, when set, is called from the reader thread after each
159	    new assistant turn with ``(turns, cumulative_tokens, peak_context_tokens,
160	    cost_usd, elapsed_seconds)``. Callers use this for live progress display
161	    and the active-run marker update.
162	    """
163	    # The prompt is the trailing positional; it must follow the value-taking
164	    # ``--model`` / ``--max-budget-usd`` / ``--debug-file`` options, not sit
165	    # between an option and its value.
166	    argv = [*claude_cmd, *_STREAM_FLAGS, "--model", model]
167	    if cost_limit_usd is not None:
168	        argv += ["--max-budget-usd", f"{cost_limit_usd:g}"]
169	    if debug_file is not None:
170	        argv += ["--debug-file", str(debug_file)]
171	    argv.append(prompt)
172	    sink = passthrough if passthrough is not None else sys.stderr
173	    accumulator = _UsageAccumulator()
174	
175	    started = time.monotonic()
176	    proc = subprocess.Popen(
177	        argv,
178	        cwd=str(cwd),
179	        stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
180	        stderr=subprocess.STDOUT,
181	        text=True,
182	        bufsize=1,
183	        start_new_session=True,
184	    )
185	
186	    # Wrap on_progress to inject elapsed_seconds computed from the start time.
187	    _progress_cb: Callable[[int, int, int, float | None], None] | None = None
188	    if on_progress is not None:
189	        def _progress_cb(turns: int, cumulative: int, peak: int, cost_usd: float | None) -> None:
190	            elapsed = time.monotonic() - started
191	            on_progress(turns, cumulative, peak, cost_usd, elapsed)
192	
193	    reader = threading.Thread(
194	        target=_drain_stream,
195	        args=(proc.stdout, accumulator, sink, _progress_cb),
196	        daemon=True,
197	    )
198	    reader.start()
199	
200	    breach = _monitor(
201	        proc,
202	        accumulator,
203	        started=started,
204	        token_limit=token_limit,
205	        time_limit_seconds=time_limit_seconds,
206	        poll_interval_seconds=poll_interval_seconds,
207	    )
208	    if breach is not None:
209	        _kill_process_group(proc)
210	        proc.wait()
211	    reader.join(timeout=_READER_JOIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS)
212	    duration = time.monotonic() - started
213	
214	    cost_usd, num_turns, session_id, is_error = accumulator.summary()
215	    return WorkerResult(
216	        exit_code=proc.returncode,
217	        breach=breach,
218	        duration_seconds=duration,
219	        model=model,
220	        usage=accumulator.usage(),
221	        cost_usd=cost_usd,
222	        num_turns=num_turns,
223	        session_id=session_id,
224	        is_error=is_error,
225	    )
226	
227	
228	def _monitor(
229	    proc: subprocess.Popen[str],
230	    accumulator: _UsageAccumulator,
231	    *,
232	    started: float,
233	    token_limit: int | None,
234	    time_limit_seconds: float | None,
235	    poll_interval_seconds: float,
236	) -> Breach | None:
237	    """Wait for the session, returning the per-issue cap it breaches (if any).
238	
239	    With both caps off there is nothing to watch for, so we block on the
240	    process directly. Otherwise we wake every ``poll_interval_seconds`` to
241	    compare the live cumulative-token tally and elapsed wall-clock against
242	    the caps; the first crossed cap is returned for the caller to kill on.
243	    Returns ``None`` when the session exits on its own first.
244	    """
245	    if token_limit is None and time_limit_seconds is None:
246	        proc.wait()
247	        return None
248	    while True:
249	        try:
250	            proc.wait(timeout=poll_interval_seconds)
251	            return None
252	        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
253	            pass
254	        if token_limit is not None:
255	            observed = accumulator.cumulative()
256	            if observed >= token_limit:
257	                return Breach("per-issue", "token", token_limit, observed)
258	        if time_limit_seconds is not None:
259	            elapsed = time.monotonic() - started
260	            if elapsed >= time_limit_seconds:
261	                return Breach("per-issue", "time", time_limit_seconds, elapsed)
262	
263	
264	def _kill_process_group(proc: subprocess.Popen[str]) -> None:
265	    """SIGKILL the worker's whole process group, reaping grandchildren.
266	
267	    No-ops if the leader has already exited (its pid — and thus the group
268	    id — is gone). SIGKILL is sent before the caller reaps the leader, so
269	    the group still exists and its id cannot have been reused.
270	    """
271	    try:
272	        pgid = os.getpgid(proc.pid)
273	    except ProcessLookupError:
274	        return
275	    try:
276	        os.killpg(pgid, signal.SIGKILL)
277	    except ProcessLookupError:
278	        pass
279	
280	
281	def _drain_stream(
282	    stream: TextIO | None,
283	    accumulator: _UsageAccumulator,
284	    sink: TextIO,
285	    on_progress: Callable[[int, int, int, float | None], None] | None = None,
286	) -> None:
287	    """Read the worker's merged output line by line until EOF.
288	
289	    Recognised JSON object events feed the usage accumulator; everything
290	    else (non-JSON diagnostics, JSON that isn't an object) is echoed to
291	    ``sink`` so the operator keeps the visibility a captured pipe would
292	    otherwise hide. ``on_progress`` is called once per new turn (deduplicated
293	    by message id) with a live usage snapshot.
294	    """
295	    if stream is None:
296	        return
297	    last_message_id: str | None = None
298	    try:
299	        for line in stream:
300	            line = line.rstrip("\n")
301	            if not line:
302	                continue
303	            try:
304	                event = json.loads(line)
305	            except json.JSONDecodeError:
306	                _echo(sink, line)
307	                continue
308	            if isinstance(event, dict):
309	                accumulator.feed(event)
310	                if on_progress is not None and event.get("type") == "assistant":
311	                    mid = (event.get("message") or {}).get("id")
312	                    if mid != last_message_id:
313	                        last_message_id = mid
314	                        snap = accumulator.live_snapshot()
315	                        on_progress(*snap)
316	            else:
317	                _echo(sink, line)
318	    finally:
319	        try:
320	            stream.close()
321	        except OSError:
322	            pass
323	
324	
325	def _echo(sink: TextIO, line: str) -> None:
326	    try:
327	        print(line, file=sink)
328	    except (OSError, ValueError):
329	        pass
330	
331	
332	def _coerce_int(value: Any) -> int:
333	    return value if isinstance(value, int) else 0
334	
335	
336	class _UsageAccumulator:
337	    """Reconstruct session usage from the streamed events.
338	
339	    Per-turn usage is keyed by ``message.id`` so a turn emitted once per
340	    content block is counted once; the last copy wins (later events carry
341	    the more complete snapshot). The ``result`` event overwrites the
342	    session-summary fields each time it is seen.
343	
344	    All mutation happens on the reader thread and all reads happen on the
345	    main thread after it is joined — but the join is bounded, so a group
346	    member that escaped the kill (e.g. via ``setsid``) could keep the
347	    reader alive past the join. The lock makes the concurrent case safe
348	    rather than a ``dictionary changed size during iteration`` crash.
349	    """
350	
351	    def __init__(self) -> None:
352	        self._lock = threading.Lock()
353	        self._turns: dict[str, dict[str, int]] = {}
354	        self._anon = 0
355	        self.cost_usd: float | None = None
356	        self.num_turns: int | None = None
357	        self.session_id: str | None = None
358	        self.is_error: bool | None = None
359	
360	    def feed(self, event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
361	        event_type = event.get("type")
362	        if event_type == "assistant":
363	            self._feed_assistant(event)
364	        elif event_type == "result":
365	            self._feed_result(event)
366	
367	    def _feed_assistant(self, event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
368	        message = event.get("message")
369	        if not isinstance(message, dict):
370	            return
371	        usage = message.get("usage")
372	        if not isinstance(usage, dict):
373	            return
374	        message_id = message.get("id")
375	        turn = {field: _coerce_int(usage.get(field)) for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS}
376	        with self._lock:
377	            if isinstance(message_id, str):
378	                key = message_id
379	            else:
380	                key = f"_anon-{self._anon}"
381	                self._anon += 1
382	            self._turns[key] = turn
383	
384	    def _feed_result(self, event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
385	        with self._lock:
386	            self.cost_usd = event.get("total_cost_usd")
387	            self.num_turns = event.get("num_turns")
388	            self.session_id = event.get("session_id")
389	            self.is_error = event.get("is_error")
390	
391	    def cumulative(self) -> int:
392	        """Live billed-token total across turns — the per-issue token cap is
393	        compared against this on every poll, so it reads under the lock and
394	        stays cheap (no peak-context / per-component breakdown)."""
395	        with self._lock:
396	            turns = list(self._turns.values())
397	        return sum(turn[field] for turn in turns for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS)
398	
399	    def live_snapshot(self) -> tuple[int, int, int, float | None]:
400	        """Current ``(turns, cumulative_tokens, peak_context_tokens, cost_usd)``.
401	
402	        Called from the reader thread after each new assistant turn. Reads
403	        under the lock so the main-thread monitor cannot see a torn state.
404	        """
405	        with self._lock:
406	            turn_count = len(self._turns)
407	            turns_list = list(self._turns.values())
408	            cost = self.cost_usd
409	        cumulative = sum(t[f] for t in turns_list for f in _TOKEN_FIELDS)
410	        peak = max(
411	            (
412	                t["input_tokens"]
413	                + t["cache_read_input_tokens"]
414	                + t["cache_creation_input_tokens"]
415	                for t in turns_list
416	            ),
417	            default=0,
418	        )
419	        return turn_count, cumulative, peak, cost
420	
421	    def usage(self) -> dict[str, int]:
422	        totals = {field: 0 for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS}
423	        peak_context = 0
424	        with self._lock:
425	            turns = list(self._turns.values())
426	        for turn in turns:
427	            for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS:
428	                totals[field] += turn[field]
429	            context = (
430	                turn["input_tokens"]
431	                + turn["cache_read_input_tokens"]
432	                + turn["cache_creation_input_tokens"]
433	            )
434	            peak_context = max(peak_context, context)
435	        return {
436	            **totals,
437	            "cumulative": sum(totals.values()),
438	            "peak_context": peak_context,
439	        }
440	
441	    def summary(self) -> tuple[float | None, int | None, str | None, bool | None]:
442	        """Read the result-event fields under the lock, as a snapshot."""
443	        with self._lock:
444	            return self.cost_usd, self.num_turns, self.session_id, self.is_error
445

file.content:
"""Spawn a streaming ``claude -p`` worker for one issue and record its usage.

The orchestrator owns the cycle loop and the Linear lifecycle; this module
owns one spawned session: how it is launched, how its token usage is
parsed off the wire, and how it is force-terminated when it overruns the
per-issue time cap.

**Why stream-json.** A bare ``claude -p`` prints only the final assistant
text; the run log then records exit code and wall-clock and nothing about
spend. ``--output-format stream-json`` (requires ``--verbose``) emits one
JSON event per line — per-turn ``assistant`` messages plus a terminal
``result`` — from which we reconstruct exactly what the session cost.

**Token accounting.** Each ``assistant`` event carries ``message.usage``,
but the same assistant message is emitted *once per content block*
(thinking, text, tool_use) — every copy repeats the identical usage under
the identical ``message.id``. Summing per event therefore double- (or
triple-) counts a turn; we key by ``message.id`` so each turn's usage is
counted exactly once. ``cumulative`` is the sum across turns of all four
token components — the real billed-token figure, dominated in long
tool-use loops by cache reads re-paid every turn. ``peak_context`` is the
largest single-turn context (input + cache_read + cache_create), i.e. how
close the session came to the context-window ceiling.

The terminal ``result`` event is authoritative for ``cost_usd``
(``total_cost_usd``), ``num_turns``, ``session_id`` and ``is_error``. Note
``result.usage`` is only the *final* turn's snapshot, not the session
total, so it is deliberately not used for the token figures — those come
from the accumulated per-turn stream, which is also what survives a kill
before any ``result`` arrives.

**Guardrails — belt and suspenders.** ``per_issue_cost_usd`` is handed to
``claude`` as ``--max-budget-usd`` so the session self-terminates on cost
(the native belt). The token and time caps are the orchestrator's
suspenders: a poll loop watches the live cumulative-token tally and the
elapsed wall-clock, and SIGKILLs the session the moment either is crossed.
Any cap passed as ``None`` is not enforced; with both token and time caps
off the worker simply waits for the session to exit on its own. A crossed
cap is reported back as a ``Breach`` so the orchestrator can name it in the
halt entry. See ``limits.py`` for the thresholds and their precedence.

**Process-group kill.** The worker is launched with
``start_new_session=True`` so it leads its own process group. On a breach
the whole group is SIGKILLed via ``os.killpg``, reaping grandchildren —
MCP servers, sub-agents — that a kill of the direct child alone would
orphan to keep burning tokens. SIGKILL (not a SIGTERM grace) is used
deliberately: a session being force-terminated past a guardrail has no
clean-shutdown work worth waiting for, and SIGKILL is the only signal a
misbehaving grandchild cannot ignore. Killing the whole group also closes
the stdout pipe those grandchildren inherited, which is what lets the
reader thread reach EOF instead of blocking forever.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import json
import os
import signal
import subprocess
import sys
import threading
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Any, Callable, TextIO

from .limits import Breach

_STREAM_FLAGS = ["--verbose", "--output-format", "stream-json"]
"""Flags that switch ``claude -p`` into line-delimited JSON event mode.
``--verbose`` is required: without it ``stream-json`` emits only the
terminal ``result`` event, dropping the per-turn ``assistant`` usage we
accumulate."""

_READER_JOIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 5.0
"""Bound on waiting for the stdout-reader thread to drain to EOF after the
process exits. The group SIGKILL closes every inherited write-end of the
pipe, so EOF normally arrives at once; the bound only guards the
pathological case of a group member that somehow escaped the kill, so the
worker returns with the usage it has rather than hanging."""

_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 1.0
"""How often the breach monitor wakes to compare the live token tally and
elapsed wall-clock against the per-issue caps. A worry-free overshoot of at
most one interval before the kill lands; one second is responsive enough for
a session measured in minutes and cheap enough to poll."""

_TOKEN_FIELDS = (
    "input_tokens",
    "output_tokens",
    "cache_creation_input_tokens",
    "cache_read_input_tokens",
)


@dataclass
class WorkerResult:
    """Outcome of one spawned session, recorded into the run-log entry.

    ``exit_code`` is the process return code (negative when killed by a
    signal, e.g. ``-9`` on a guardrail SIGKILL). ``breach`` is the branch
    selector the orchestrator reads: ``None`` when the session ran to its
    own exit, otherwise the per-issue token or time cap that was crossed,
    carrying the cap and the value observed at kill time. ``usage`` carries
    the four token components plus ``cumulative`` and ``peak_context``.
    ``cost_usd`` / ``num_turns`` / ``session_id`` / ``is_error`` come from
    the terminal ``result`` event and are ``None`` when the session was
    killed before one arrived.
    """

    exit_code: int
    breach: Breach | None
    duration_seconds: float
    model: str
    usage: dict[str, int]
    cost_usd: float | None
    num_turns: int | None
    session_id: str | None
    is_error: bool | None


def run_issue(
    *,
    claude_cmd: list[str],
    model: str,
    prompt: str,
    cwd: Path,
    token_limit: int | None,
    time_limit_seconds: float | None,
    cost_limit_usd: float | None,
    passthrough: TextIO | None = None,
    debug_file: Path | None = None,
    poll_interval_seconds: float = _POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS,
    on_progress: Callable[[int, int, int, float | None, float], None] | None = None,
) -> WorkerResult:
    """Spawn one streaming ``claude -p`` session and return its usage.

    ``claude_cmd`` is the base argv (``["claude", "-p",
    "--dangerously-skip-permissions"]``); the streaming flags, ``--model``,
    ``--max-budget-usd`` (when ``cost_limit_usd`` is set), and the prompt are
    appended here. The session runs in its own process group; if its
    cumulative tokens cross ``token_limit`` or its wall-clock crosses
    ``time_limit_seconds`` the whole group is killed and the returned
    ``WorkerResult.breach`` names the cap. A limit passed as ``None`` is not
    enforced. ``cost_limit_usd`` is enforced by ``claude`` itself, not here.

    Non-JSON lines on the merged stdout/stderr stream (claude warnings,
    hook stderr) are written to ``passthrough`` (default ``sys.stderr``) so
    the operator still sees diagnostics; recognised JSON events are
    consumed by the usage parser.

    ``debug_file``, when set, is passed to ``claude`` as ``--debug-file`` so
    the session writes its startup diagnostics — which settings sources,
    plugins, MCP servers, and hooks initialised — to that path. Debug logs
    go to the file, not stderr, so the merged stream the usage parser reads
    is unaffected. Opt-in only; ``None`` (the default) passes no flag and the
    session behaves exactly as before. See ``docs/design-decisions.md`` §10.

    ``on_progress``, when set, is called from the reader thread after each
    new assistant turn with ``(turns, cumulative_tokens, peak_context_tokens,
    cost_usd, elapsed_seconds)``. Callers use this for live progress display
    and the active-run marker update.
    """
    # The prompt is the trailing positional; it must follow the value-taking
    # ``--model`` / ``--max-budget-usd`` / ``--debug-file`` options, not sit
    # between an option and its value.
    argv = [*claude_cmd, *_STREAM_FLAGS, "--model", model]
    if cost_limit_usd is not None:
        argv += ["--max-budget-usd", f"{cost_limit_usd:g}"]
    if debug_file is not None:
        argv += ["--debug-file", str(debug_file)]
    argv.append(prompt)
    sink = passthrough if passthrough is not None else sys.stderr
    accumulator = _UsageAccumulator()

    started = time.monotonic()
    proc = subprocess.Popen(
        argv,
        cwd=str(cwd),
        stdout=subprocess.PIPE,
        stderr=subprocess.STDOUT,
        text=True,
        bufsize=1,
        start_new_session=True,
    )

    # Wrap on_progress to inject elapsed_seconds computed from the start time.
    _progress_cb: Callable[[int, int, int, float | None], None] | None = None
    if on_progress is not None:
        def _progress_cb(turns: int, cumulative: int, peak: int, cost_usd: float | None) -> None:
            elapsed = time.monotonic() - started
            on_progress(turns, cumulative, peak, cost_usd, elapsed)

    reader = threading.Thread(
        target=_drain_stream,
        args=(proc.stdout, accumulator, sink, _progress_cb),
        daemon=True,
    )
    reader.start()

    breach = _monitor(
        proc,
        accumulator,
        started=started,
        token_limit=token_limit,
        time_limit_seconds=time_limit_seconds,
        poll_interval_seconds=poll_interval_seconds,
    )
    if breach is not None:
        _kill_process_group(proc)
        proc.wait()
    reader.join(timeout=_READER_JOIN_TIMEOUT_SECONDS)
    duration = time.monotonic() - started

    cost_usd, num_turns, session_id, is_error = accumulator.summary()
    return WorkerResult(
        exit_code=proc.returncode,
        breach=breach,
        duration_seconds=duration,
        model=model,
        usage=accumulator.usage(),
        cost_usd=cost_usd,
        num_turns=num_turns,
        session_id=session_id,
        is_error=is_error,
    )


def _monitor(
    proc: subprocess.Popen[str],
    accumulator: _UsageAccumulator,
    *,
    started: float,
    token_limit: int | None,
    time_limit_seconds: float | None,
    poll_interval_seconds: float,
) -> Breach | None:
    """Wait for the session, returning the per-issue cap it breaches (if any).

    With both caps off there is nothing to watch for, so we block on the
    process directly. Otherwise we wake every ``poll_interval_seconds`` to
    compare the live cumulative-token tally and elapsed wall-clock against
    the caps; the first crossed cap is returned for the caller to kill on.
    Returns ``None`` when the session exits on its own first.
    """
    if token_limit is None and time_limit_seconds is None:
        proc.wait()
        return None
    while True:
        try:
            proc.wait(timeout=poll_interval_seconds)
            return None
        except subprocess.TimeoutExpired:
            pass
        if token_limit is not None:
            observed = accumulator.cumulative()
            if observed >= token_limit:
                return Breach("per-issue", "token", token_limit, observed)
        if time_limit_seconds is not None:
            elapsed = time.monotonic() - started
            if elapsed >= time_limit_seconds:
                return Breach("per-issue", "time", time_limit_seconds, elapsed)


def _kill_process_group(proc: subprocess.Popen[str]) -> None:
    """SIGKILL the worker's whole process group, reaping grandchildren.

    No-ops if the leader has already exited (its pid — and thus the group
    id — is gone). SIGKILL is sent before the caller reaps the leader, so
    the group still exists and its id cannot have been reused.
    """
    try:
        pgid = os.getpgid(proc.pid)
    except ProcessLookupError:
        return
    try:
        os.killpg(pgid, signal.SIGKILL)
    except ProcessLookupError:
        pass


def _drain_stream(
    stream: TextIO | None,
    accumulator: _UsageAccumulator,
    sink: TextIO,
    on_progress: Callable[[int, int, int, float | None], None] | None = None,
) -> None:
    """Read the worker's merged output line by line until EOF.

    Recognised JSON object events feed the usage accumulator; everything
    else (non-JSON diagnostics, JSON that isn't an object) is echoed to
    ``sink`` so the operator keeps the visibility a captured pipe would
    otherwise hide. ``on_progress`` is called once per new turn (deduplicated
    by message id) with a live usage snapshot.
    """
    if stream is None:
        return
    last_message_id: str | None = None
    try:
        for line in stream:
            line = line.rstrip("\n")
            if not line:
                continue
            try:
                event = json.loads(line)
            except json.JSONDecodeError:
                _echo(sink, line)
                continue
            if isinstance(event, dict):
                accumulator.feed(event)
                if on_progress is not None and event.get("type") == "assistant":
                    mid = (event.get("message") or {}).get("id")
                    if mid != last_message_id:
                        last_message_id = mid
                        snap = accumulator.live_snapshot()
                        on_progress(*snap)
            else:
                _echo(sink, line)
    finally:
        try:
            stream.close()
        except OSError:
            pass


def _echo(sink: TextIO, line: str) -> None:
    try:
        print(line, file=sink)
    except (OSError, ValueError):
        pass


def _coerce_int(value: Any) -> int:
    return value if isinstance(value, int) else 0


class _UsageAccumulator:
    """Reconstruct session usage from the streamed events.

    Per-turn usage is keyed by ``message.id`` so a turn emitted once per
    content block is counted once; the last copy wins (later events carry
    the more complete snapshot). The ``result`` event overwrites the
    session-summary fields each time it is seen.

    All mutation happens on the reader thread and all reads happen on the
    main thread after it is joined — but the join is bounded, so a group
    member that escaped the kill (e.g. via ``setsid``) could keep the
    reader alive past the join. The lock makes the concurrent case safe
    rather than a ``dictionary changed size during iteration`` crash.
    """

    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self._lock = threading.Lock()
        self._turns: dict[str, dict[str, int]] = {}
        self._anon = 0
        self.cost_usd: float | None = None
        self.num_turns: int | None = None
        self.session_id: str | None = None
        self.is_error: bool | None = None

    def feed(self, event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
        event_type = event.get("type")
        if event_type == "assistant":
            self._feed_assistant(event)
        elif event_type == "result":
            self._feed_result(event)

    def _feed_assistant(self, event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
        message = event.get("message")
        if not isinstance(message, dict):
            return
        usage = message.get("usage")
        if not isinstance(usage, dict):
            return
        message_id = message.get("id")
        turn = {field: _coerce_int(usage.get(field)) for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS}
        with self._lock:
            if isinstance(message_id, str):
                key = message_id
            else:
                key = f"_anon-{self._anon}"
                self._anon += 1
            self._turns[key] = turn

    def _feed_result(self, event: dict[str, Any]) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            self.cost_usd = event.get("total_cost_usd")
            self.num_turns = event.get("num_turns")
            self.session_id = event.get("session_id")
            self.is_error = event.get("is_error")

    def cumulative(self) -> int:
        """Live billed-token total across turns — the per-issue token cap is
        compared against this on every poll, so it reads under the lock and
        stays cheap (no peak-context / per-component breakdown)."""
        with self._lock:
            turns = list(self._turns.values())
        return sum(turn[field] for turn in turns for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS)

    def live_snapshot(self) -> tuple[int, int, int, float | None]:
        """Current ``(turns, cumulative_tokens, peak_context_tokens, cost_usd)``.

        Called from the reader thread after each new assistant turn. Reads
        under the lock so the main-thread monitor cannot see a torn state.
        """
        with self._lock:
            turn_count = len(self._turns)
            turns_list = list(self._turns.values())
            cost = self.cost_usd
        cumulative = sum(t[f] for t in turns_list for f in _TOKEN_FIELDS)
        peak = max(
            (
                t["input_tokens"]
                + t["cache_read_input_tokens"]
                + t["cache_creation_input_tokens"]
                for t in turns_list
            ),
            default=0,
        )
        return turn_count, cumulative, peak, cost

    def usage(self) -> dict[str, int]:
        totals = {field: 0 for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS}
        peak_context = 0
        with self._lock:
            turns = list(self._turns.values())
        for turn in turns:
            for field in _TOKEN_FIELDS:
                totals[field] += turn[field]
            context = (
                turn["input_tokens"]
                + turn["cache_read_input_tokens"]
                + turn["cache_creation_input_tokens"]
            )
            peak_context = max(peak_context, context)
        return {
            **totals,
            "cumulative": sum(totals.values()),
            "peak_context": peak_context,
        }

    def summary(self) -> tuple[float | None, int | None, str | None, bool | None]:
        """Read the result-event fields under the lock, as a snapshot."""
        with self._lock:
            return self.cost_usd, self.num_turns, self.session_id, self.is_error

1	"""Minimal Linear GraphQL client.
2	
3	Reads ``LINEAR_API_KEY`` from the environment. The CLI entrypoint
4	(``cli.main``) loads ``.env`` from the drain-cycle repo root before this
5	module is exercised; a shell-exported value still takes precedence.
6	Single-team scope: hardcoded to the ``Personal`` team per README §1.
7	"""
8	from __future__ import annotations
9	
10	import os
11	from dataclasses import dataclass
12	from typing import Any
13	
14	import httpx
15	
16	_ENDPOINT = "https://api.linear.app/graphql"
17	_TEAM_NAME = "Personal"
18	_PENDING_STATE_TYPES = ["backlog", "unstarted"]
19	_RESOLVED_STATE_TYPES = {"completed", "canceled"}
20	
21	
22	@dataclass(frozen=True)
23	class ExecutionPlan:
24	    """Result of ``_plan``: runnable issues in topo order + deferred issues."""
25	    order: list[dict[str, Any]]   # issues to spawn, in execution order
26	    deferred: list[dict[str, Any]]  # each {"issue", "blocker_identifier", "blocker_state_type"}
27	
28	
29	class DependencyCycleError(RuntimeError):
30	    """Raised when the blocks graph among pending issues contains a cycle."""
31	    def __init__(self, identifiers: list[str]) -> None:
32	        self.identifiers = list(identifiers)
33	        super().__init__(f"Dependency cycle among: {', '.join(identifiers)}")
34	
35	
36	def _post(query: str, variables: dict[str, Any] | None = None) -> dict[str, Any]:
37	    key = os.environ.get("LINEAR_API_KEY")
38	    if not key:
39	        raise RuntimeError("LINEAR_API_KEY is not set")
40	    resp = httpx.post(
41	        _ENDPOINT,
42	        headers={"Authorization": key, "Content-Type": "application/json"},
43	        json={"query": query, "variables": variables or {}},
44	        timeout=30.0,
45	    )
46	    resp.raise_for_status()
47	    body = resp.json()
48	    if "errors" in body:
49	        raise RuntimeError(f"Linear GraphQL errors: {body['errors']}")
50	    return body["data"]
51	
52	
53	def current_cycle_id() -> str:
54	    """Return the active cycle id for the configured team."""
55	    data = _post(
56	        """
57	        query CurrentCycle($name: String!) {
58	          teams(filter: { name: { eq: $name } }) {
59	            nodes {
60	              id
61	              activeCycle { id }
62	            }
63	          }
64	        }
65	        """,
66	        {"name": _TEAM_NAME},
67	    )
68	    nodes = data["teams"]["nodes"]
69	    if not nodes:
70	        raise RuntimeError(f"Linear team {_TEAM_NAME!r} not found")
71	    cycle = nodes[0].get("activeCycle")
72	    if not cycle:
73	        raise RuntimeError(f"Linear team {_TEAM_NAME!r} has no active cycle")
74	    return cycle["id"]
75	
76	
77	def _plan(issues: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> ExecutionPlan:
78	    """Topological sort over ``issues`` respecting blocks/blocked-by.
79	
80	    Returns an ``ExecutionPlan`` with ``order`` (runnable, topo-sorted by
81	    ``(sortOrder, id)`` tiebreak) and ``deferred`` (issues skipped because
82	    an external unresolved blocker exists, or because a blocking issue in
83	    the drain was itself deferred — cascade).
84	
85	    Raises ``DependencyCycleError`` when the intra-set blocks graph contains
86	    a cycle (self-loops included).
87	    """
88	    P: set[str] = {i["id"] for i in issues}
89	    by_id: dict[str, dict[str, Any]] = {i["id"]: i for i in issues}
90	
91	    # Build de-duped intra-P edges: blocker_id → set of blocked_ids
92	    children: dict[str, set[str]] = {i["id"]: set() for i in issues}
93	    # Per-issue unique set of intra-P blocker ids (for cascade check + in-degree)
94	    intra_blockers: dict[str, set[str]] = {i["id"]: set() for i in issues}
95	    indegree: dict[str, int] = {i["id"]: 0 for i in issues}
96	
97	    for issue in issues:
98	        for blocker in issue.get("blockers", []):
99	            bid = blocker["id"]
100	            if bid in P:
101	                if bid not in intra_blockers[issue["id"]]:
102	                    intra_blockers[issue["id"]].add(bid)
103	                    indegree[issue["id"]] += 1
104	                    children[bid].add(issue["id"])
105	
106	    # Kahn's algorithm: ready = in-degree-0 issues, sorted by (sortOrder, id)
107	    ready: list[dict[str, Any]] = sorted(
108	        [i for i in issues if indegree[i["id"]] == 0],
109	        key=lambda i: (i["sortOrder"], i["id"]),
110	    )
111	
112	    order: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
113	    deferred: list[dict[str, Any]] = []
114	    deferred_ids: set[str] = set()
115	
116	    while ready:
117	        issue = ready.pop(0)
118	        iid = issue["id"]
119	
120	        # Cascade: any intra-P blocker already deferred → defer this too
121	        cascade_blocker = next(
122	            (by_id[bid] for bid in intra_blockers[iid] if bid in deferred_ids),
123	            None,
124	        )
125	        if cascade_blocker is not None:
126	            deferred.append({
127	                "issue": issue,
128	                "blocker_identifier": cascade_blocker["identifier"],
129	                "blocker_state_type": cascade_blocker["state"]["type"],
130	            })
131	            deferred_ids.add(iid)
132	        else:
133	            # External unresolved blocker → defer
134	            ext_blocker = next(
135	                (
136	                    b for b in issue.get("blockers", [])
137	                    if b["id"] not in P and b["state_type"] not in _RESOLVED_STATE_TYPES
138	                ),
139	                None,
140	            )
141	            if ext_blocker is not None:
142	                deferred.append({
143	                    "issue": issue,
144	                    "blocker_identifier": ext_blocker["identifier"],
145	                    "blocker_state_type": ext_blocker["state_type"],
146	                })
147	                deferred_ids.add(iid)
148	            else:
149	                order.append(issue)
150	
151	        # Decrement children regardless (deferred nodes still unblock their children
152	        # in the graph so cycle detection remains accurate)
153	        for child_id in children[iid]:
154	            indegree[child_id] -= 1
155	            if indegree[child_id] == 0:
156	                child = by_id[child_id]
157	                # Insert maintaining (sortOrder, id) order
158	                idx = 0
159	                key = (child["sortOrder"], child["id"])
160	                while idx < len(ready) and (ready[idx]["sortOrder"], ready[idx]["id"]) <= key:
161	                    idx += 1
162	                ready.insert(idx, child)
163	
164	    cycle_nodes = [i for i in issues if indegree[i["id"]] > 0]
165	    if cycle_nodes:
166	        raise DependencyCycleError([i["identifier"] for i in cycle_nodes])
167	
168	    return ExecutionPlan(order=order, deferred=deferred)
169	
170	
171	def _label_name(node: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
172	    """Render a label node in its prefix form.
173	
174	    A label in a Linear label *group* arrives as a leaf ``name`` with a
175	    ``parent`` group; downstream resolvers match on a ``<group>:`` prefix
176	    (``repo:``, ``model:``), so a grouped label renders as
177	    ``"<group>:<name>"``. An ungrouped label keeps its bare name, which
178	    already carries any literal prefix typed into the label name.
179	    """
180	    parent = node.get("parent")
181	    if parent:
182	        return f"{parent['name']}:{node['name']}"
183	    return node["name"]
184	
185	
186	def pending_issues(cycle_id: str) -> ExecutionPlan:
187	    """Return an ``ExecutionPlan`` for every Todo/Backlog issue in the cycle.
188	
189	    No pagination: personal cycles fit comfortably in one page. If a cycle
190	    ever exceeds 100 pending issues, that's a planning problem, not a tool
191	    problem (see ``PRODUCT_RULES`` Rule A5 — focus is the multiplier).
192	
193	    Post-processing flattens two wire-shape fields:
194	    - ``labels { nodes { name parent { name } } }`` → ``labels: list[str]``
195	      (grouped labels rendered as ``"<group>:<name>"``)
196	    - ``inverseRelations`` filtered to ``type == "blocks"``
197	      → ``blockers: list[{id, identifier, state_type}]``; raw key removed.
198	    """
199	    data = _post(
200	        """
201	        query CyclePending($cycleId: ID!, $stateTypes: [String!]!) {
202	          issues(
203	            filter: {
204	              cycle: { id: { eq: $cycleId } }
205	              state: { type: { in: $stateTypes } }
206	            }
207	            first: 100
208	          ) {
209	            nodes {
210	              id
211	              identifier
212	              title
213	              description
214	              sortOrder
215	              state { type name }
216	              labels { nodes { name parent { name } } }
217	              inverseRelations { nodes { type issue { id identifier state { type } } } }
218	            }
219	          }
220	        }
221	        """,
222	        {"cycleId": cycle_id, "stateTypes": _PENDING_STATE_TYPES},
223	    )
224	    issues = data["issues"]["nodes"]
225	    for issue in issues:
226	        issue["labels"] = [_label_name(node) for node in issue["labels"]["nodes"]]
227	        issue["blockers"] = [
228	            {
229	                "id": node["issue"]["id"],
230	                "identifier": node["issue"]["identifier"],
231	                "state_type": node["issue"]["state"]["type"],
232	            }
233	            for node in issue["inverseRelations"]["nodes"]
234	            if node["type"] == "blocks"
235	        ]
236	        del issue["inverseRelations"]
237	    return _plan(issues)
238	
239	
240	def get_issue(issue_id: str) -> dict[str, Any]:
241	    """Re-fetch an issue's current state by id."""
242	    data = _post(
243	        """
244	        query Issue($id: String!) {
245	          issue(id: $id) {
246	            id
247	            identifier
248	            title
249	            state { type name }
250	          }
251	        }
252	        """,
253	        {"id": issue_id},
254	    )
255	    issue = data["issue"]
256	    if issue is None:
257	        raise RuntimeError(f"Linear issue {issue_id!r} not found")
258	    return issue
259	
260	
261	def set_state(issue_id: str, state_name: str) -> None:
262	    """Transition an issue to the named workflow state.
263	
264	    Resolves the state ID by name against the configured team and then issues
265	    an ``issueUpdate`` mutation. No caching — at cycle scale (≤ ~15 issues)
266	    the extra round-trip is irrelevant and the simpler code is easier to test.
267	    """
268	    state_id = _resolve_state_id(state_name)
269	    data = _post(
270	        """
271	        mutation IssueSetState($id: String!, $input: IssueUpdateInput!) {
272	          issueUpdate(id: $id, input: $input) {
273	            success
274	          }
275	        }
276	        """,
277	        {"id": issue_id, "input": {"stateId": state_id}},
278	    )
279	    if not data["issueUpdate"]["success"]:
280	        raise RuntimeError(
281	            f"Linear issueUpdate failed for {issue_id!r} → {state_name!r}"
282	        )
283	
284	
285	def _resolve_state_id(state_name: str) -> str:
286	    data = _post(
287	        """
288	        query WorkflowState($team: String!, $name: String!) {
289	          workflowStates(
290	            filter: {
291	              team: { name: { eq: $team } }
292	              name: { eq: $name }
293	            }
294	            first: 1
295	          ) {
296	            nodes { id }
297	          }
298	        }
299	        """,
300	        {"team": _TEAM_NAME, "name": state_name},
301	    )
302	    nodes = data["workflowStates"]["nodes"]
303	    if not nodes:
304	        raise RuntimeError(
305	            f"Linear workflow state {state_name!r} not found for team {_TEAM_NAME!r}"
306	        )
307	    return nodes[0]["id"]
308

1	"""Thin wrapper around ``git worktree``.
2	
3	Each issue gets ``.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/`` branched off ``main`` per
4	README §3, used once, then removed on Done.
5	
6	``git worktree`` stderr is captured and surfaced in the raised
7	``RuntimeError`` on failure. The orchestrator's pre-spawn try/except
8	threads the message into the runlog's ``halt_reason`` so the operator
9	sees git's actual diagnostic (dirty tree, branch already exists,
10	missing ``main``) rather than just a non-zero exit code.
11	"""
12	from __future__ import annotations
13	
14	import os
15	import subprocess
16	from pathlib import Path
17	from typing import Iterable
18	
19	BASE_BRANCH = "main"
20	WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"
21	
22	
23	def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
24	    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.
25	
26	    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
27	    """
28	    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
29	    _run_git(
30	        ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
31	        cwd=repo,
32	    )
33	    return worktree_path
34	
35	
36	def link_project_config(
37	    repo: Path, worktree_path: Path, names: Iterable[str]
38	) -> list[Path]:
39	    """Symlink gitignored project-scoped config from ``repo`` into the worktree.
40	
41	    A git worktree checks out only tracked files, so gitignored project config
42	    (``.claude/`` settings/hooks/agents/skills, a root ``.mcp.json``) is absent.
43	    Linking the repo's real entries in gives a worker the same settings, hooks,
44	    agents, skills, and MCP config as an interactive session at the repo root —
45	    and because the link points at the live dir, a stateful hook reads and
46	    writes the repo's actual config exactly as a non-worktree run would.
47	
48	    For each name: skip it if absent in ``repo`` (a clean no-op for repos
49	    without that config) or if something already occupies that path in the
50	    worktree (a tracked entry git checked out, or a pre-existing link). The
51	    check uses ``os.path.lexists`` so a dangling link counts as present and is
52	    never clobbered. Returns the links created.
53	    """
54	    created: list[Path] = []
55	    repo = repo.resolve()
56	    for name in names:
57	        source = repo / name
58	        if not source.exists():
59	            continue
60	        link = worktree_path / name
61	        if os.path.lexists(link):
62	            continue
63	        os.symlink(source, link)
64	        created.append(link)
65	    return created
66	
67	
68	def remove(repo: Path, worktree_path: Path) -> None:
69	    """Remove a worktree previously created by :func:`add`."""
70	    _run_git(["worktree", "remove", str(worktree_path)], cwd=repo)
71	
72	
73	def _run_git(args: list[str], *, cwd: Path) -> None:
74	    result = subprocess.run(
75	        ["git", *args],
76	        cwd=cwd,
77	        check=False,
78	        capture_output=True,
79	        text=True,
80	    )
81	    if result.returncode != 0:
82	        raise RuntimeError(
83	            f"git {' '.join(args)} failed (exit {result.returncode}): "
84	            f"{result.stderr.strip() or result.stdout.strip() or '<no output>'}"
85	        )

file.content:
"""Thin wrapper around ``git worktree``.

Each issue gets ``.worktrees/<issue-identifier>/`` branched off ``main`` per
README §3, used once, then removed on Done.

``git worktree`` stderr is captured and surfaced in the raised
``RuntimeError`` on failure. The orchestrator's pre-spawn try/except
threads the message into the runlog's ``halt_reason`` so the operator
sees git's actual diagnostic (dirty tree, branch already exists,
missing ``main``) rather than just a non-zero exit code.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import os
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

BASE_BRANCH = "main"
WORKTREE_DIR = ".worktrees"


def add(repo: Path, identifier: str) -> Path:
    """Create a worktree branched off ``main`` for ``identifier``.

    Returns the absolute path to the new worktree.
    """
    worktree_path = repo / WORKTREE_DIR / identifier
    _run_git(
        ["worktree", "add", "-b", identifier, str(worktree_path), BASE_BRANCH],
        cwd=repo,
    )
    return worktree_path


def link_project_config(
    repo: Path, worktree_path: Path, names: Iterable[str]
) -> list[Path]:
    """Symlink gitignored project-scoped config from ``repo`` into the worktree.

    A git worktree checks out only tracked files, so gitignored project config
    (``.claude/`` settings/hooks/agents/skills, a root ``.mcp.json``) is absent.
    Linking the repo's real entries in gives a worker the same settings, hooks,
    agents, skills, and MCP config as an interactive session at the repo root —
    and because the link points at the live dir, a stateful hook reads and
    writes the repo's actual config exactly as a non-worktree run would.

    For each name: skip it if absent in ``repo`` (a clean no-op for repos
    without that config) or if something already occupies that path in the
    worktree (a tracked entry git checked out, or a pre-existing link). The
    check uses ``os.path.lexists`` so a dangling link counts as present and is
    never clobbered. Returns the links created.
    """
    created: list[Path] = []
    repo = repo.resolve()
    for name in names:
        source = repo / name
        if not source.exists():
            continue
        link = worktree_path / name
        if os.path.lexists(link):
            continue
        os.symlink(source, link)
        created.append(link)
    return created


def remove(repo: Path, worktree_path: Path) -> None:
    """Remove a worktree previously created by :func:`add`."""
    _run_git(["worktree", "remove", str(worktree_path)], cwd=repo)


def _run_git(args: list[str], *, cwd: Path) -> None:
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", *args],
        cwd=cwd,
        check=False,
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        raise RuntimeError(
            f"git {' '.join(args)} failed (exit {result.returncode}): "
            f"{result.stderr.strip() or result.stdout.strip() or '<no output>'}"
        )

content.content:
1	# SDK Setup by Language
2	
3	Complete OpenTelemetry SDK setup instructions for each language, configured to send
4	traces to Honeycomb.
5	
6	## Environment Variables (All Languages)
7	
8	```bash
9	export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://api.honeycomb.io"
10	export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="x-honeycomb-team=YOUR_API_KEY"
11	export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="your-service-name"
12	```
13	
14	EU endpoint: `https://api.eu1.honeycomb.io`
15	
16	## Go
17	
18	### Dependencies
19	```bash
20	go get go.opentelemetry.io/otel \
21	       go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/trace \
22	       go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace/otlptracehttp
23	```
24	
25	### Auto-instrumentation libraries
26	```bash
27	go get go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/net/http/otelhttp
28	go get go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/google.golang.org/grpc/otelgrpc
29	```
30	
31	### Notes
32	- Use `otelhttp.NewHandler()` to wrap HTTP handlers
33	- Use `otelgrpc.UnaryServerInterceptor()` for gRPC
34	- SDK reads env vars automatically
35	
36	## Python
37	
38	### Dependencies
39	```bash
40	pip install opentelemetry-sdk \
41	            opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-http \
42	            opentelemetry-instrumentation-flask \
43	            opentelemetry-instrumentation-requests \
44	            opentelemetry-instrumentation-sqlalchemy
45	```
46	
47	### Auto-instrumentation (recommended)
48	```bash
49	opentelemetry-instrument python app.py
50	```
51	
52	### Programmatic setup
53	```python
54	from opentelemetry import trace
55	from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
56	from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
57	from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
58	from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource
59	
60	resource = Resource.create({"service.name": "your-service"})
61	provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
62	processor = BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter())
63	provider.add_span_processor(processor)
64	trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
65	```
66	
67	## Node.js
68	
69	### Dependencies
70	```bash
71	npm install @opentelemetry/sdk-node \
72	            @opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-http \
73	            @opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node
74	```
75	
76	### Setup (tracing.js — require before app)
77	```javascript
78	const { NodeSDK } = require("@opentelemetry/sdk-node");
79	const { OTLPTraceExporter } = require("@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-http");
80	const { getNodeAutoInstrumentations } = require("@opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node");
81	
82	const sdk = new NodeSDK({
83	  traceExporter: new OTLPTraceExporter(),
84	  instrumentations: [getNodeAutoInstrumentations()],
85	});
86	sdk.start();
87	```
88	
89	### Run
90	```bash
91	node --require ./tracing.js app.js
92	```
93	
94	## Java
95	
96	### Java Agent (recommended — zero code changes)
97	```bash
98	# Download agent jar
99	curl -L -o opentelemetry-javaagent.jar \
100	  https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-java-instrumentation/releases/latest/download/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar
101	
102	# Run with agent
103	java -javaagent:opentelemetry-javaagent.jar \
104	     -Dotel.exporter.otlp.endpoint=https://api.honeycomb.io \
105	     -Dotel.exporter.otlp.REDACTED \
106	     -Dotel.service.name=your-service \
107	     -jar your-app.jar
108	```
109	
110	### Notes
111	- Java agent auto-instruments most frameworks (Spring, Servlet, JDBC, etc.)
112	- No code changes required for basic tracing
113	- Add custom spans via OTel API for business logic
114	
115	## Ruby
116	
117	### Dependencies
118	```ruby
119	# Gemfile
120	gem "opentelemetry-sdk"
121	gem "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp"
122	gem "opentelemetry-instrumentation-all"
123	```
124	
125	### Setup
126	```ruby
127	require "opentelemetry/sdk"
128	require "opentelemetry/exporter/otlp"
129	require "opentelemetry/instrumentation/all"
130	
131	OpenTelemetry::SDK.configure do |c|
132	  c.service_name = "your-service"
133	  c.use_all  # auto-instrument all supported libraries
134	end
135	```

141	dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting
142	dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Exporter.OpenTelemetryProtocol
143	dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.AspNetCore
144	dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.Http
145	```

149	builder.Services.AddOpenTelemetry()
150	    .WithTracing(tracing => tracing
151	        .AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()
152	        .AddHttpClientInstrumentation()
153	        .AddOtlpExporter());
154	```

156	## Rust

160	[dependencies]
161	opentelemetry = "0.21"
162	opentelemetry-otlp = { version = "0.14", features = ["http-proto"] }
163	opentelemetry_sdk = { version = "0.21", features = ["rt-tokio"] }
164	```
165	
166	### Notes
167	- Rust uses OTLP exporter directly
168	- No auto-instrumentation; all spans are manual
169	- Use `tracing` crate with `tracing-opentelemetry` for ergonomic instrumentation

file.content:
# SDK Setup by Language

Complete OpenTelemetry SDK setup instructions for each language, configured to send
traces to Honeycomb.

## Environment Variables (All Languages)

```bash
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://api.honeycomb.io"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_HEADERS="x-honeycomb-team=YOUR_API_KEY"
export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="your-service-name"
```

EU endpoint: `https://api.eu1.honeycomb.io`

## Go

### Dependencies
```bash
go get go.opentelemetry.io/otel \
       go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/trace \
       go.opentelemetry.io/otel/exporters/otlp/otlptrace/otlptracehttp
```

### Auto-instrumentation libraries
```bash
go get go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/net/http/otelhttp
go get go.opentelemetry.io/contrib/instrumentation/google.golang.org/grpc/otelgrpc
```

### Notes
- Use `otelhttp.NewHandler()` to wrap HTTP handlers
- Use `otelgrpc.UnaryServerInterceptor()` for gRPC
- SDK reads env vars automatically

## Python

### Dependencies
```bash
pip install opentelemetry-sdk \
            opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-http \
            opentelemetry-instrumentation-flask \
            opentelemetry-instrumentation-requests \
            opentelemetry-instrumentation-sqlalchemy
```

### Auto-instrumentation (recommended)
```bash
opentelemetry-instrument python app.py
```

### Programmatic setup
```python
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource

resource = Resource.create({"service.name": "your-service"})
provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
processor = BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter())
provider.add_span_processor(processor)
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
```

## Node.js

### Dependencies
```bash
npm install @opentelemetry/sdk-node \
            @opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-http \
            @opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node
```

### Setup (tracing.js — require before app)
```javascript
const { NodeSDK } = require("@opentelemetry/sdk-node");
const { OTLPTraceExporter } = require("@opentelemetry/exporter-trace-otlp-http");
const { getNodeAutoInstrumentations } = require("@opentelemetry/auto-instrumentations-node");

const sdk = new NodeSDK({
  traceExporter: new OTLPTraceExporter(),
  instrumentations: [getNodeAutoInstrumentations()],
});
sdk.start();
```

### Run
```bash
node --require ./tracing.js app.js
```

## Java

### Java Agent (recommended — zero code changes)
```bash
# Download agent jar
curl -L -o opentelemetry-javaagent.jar \
  https://github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-java-instrumentation/releases/latest/download/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar

# Run with agent
java -javaagent:opentelemetry-javaagent.jar \
     -Dotel.exporter.otlp.endpoint=https://api.honeycomb.io \
     -Dotel.exporter.otlp.REDACTED \
     -Dotel.service.name=your-service \
     -jar your-app.jar
```

### Notes
- Java agent auto-instruments most frameworks (Spring, Servlet, JDBC, etc.)
- No code changes required for basic tracing
- Add custom spans via OTel API for business logic

## Ruby

### Dependencies
```ruby
# Gemfile
gem "opentelemetry-sdk"
gem "opentelemetry-exporter-otlp"
gem "opentelemetry-instrumentation-all"
```

### Setup
```ruby
require "opentelemetry/sdk"
require "opentelemetry/exporter/otlp"
require "opentelemetry/instrumentation/all"

OpenTelemetry::SDK.configure do |c|
  c.service_name = "your-service"
  c.use_all  # auto-instrument all supported libraries
end
```

## .NET

### Dependencies
```bash
dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Extensions.Hosting
dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Exporter.OpenTelemetryProtocol
dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.AspNetCore
dotnet add package OpenTelemetry.Instrumentation.Http
```

### Setup (Program.cs)
```csharp
builder.Services.AddOpenTelemetry()
    .WithTracing(tracing => tracing
        .AddAspNetCoreInstrumentation()
        .AddHttpClientInstrumentation()
        .AddOtlpExporter());
```

## Rust

### Dependencies (Cargo.toml)
```toml
[dependencies]
opentelemetry = "0.21"
opentelemetry-otlp = { version = "0.14", features = ["http-proto"] }
opentelemetry_sdk = { version = "0.21", features = ["rt-tokio"] }
```

### Notes
- Rust uses OTLP exporter directly
- No auto-instrumentation; all spans are manual
- Use `tracing` crate with `tracing-opentelemetry` for ergonomic instrumentation

1	"""Per-issue worker-model resolution.
2	
3	Workers default to a cost-efficient model. A spawned ``claude -p`` session
4	inherits whatever model the operator has globally pinned, which can be the
5	most expensive one — so unattended cycles default to Sonnet here and let an
6	individual issue opt up (or down) via a ``model:<alias>`` Linear label,
7	mirroring the ``repo:<name>`` mechanism in ``repos.py``.
8	
9	Resolution is deliberately lenient and never raises: a worker always has a
10	safe model to run, so any ambiguity falls back to the default rather than
11	halting an unattended cycle. The model actually used is recorded in the run
12	log, so a mis-labelled issue surfaces after the fact instead of stalling.
13	
14	* No ``model:`` label → ``DEFAULT_MODEL``.
15	* Exactly one label → its value, mapped through ``_ALIASES`` if known,
16	  otherwise passed through verbatim (``claude --model`` validates it).
17	* More than one label → ``DEFAULT_MODEL`` (ambiguous intent; the cheap,
18	  safe default wins).
19	"""
20	from __future__ import annotations
21	
22	from typing import Any
23	
24	_MODEL_LABEL_PREFIX = "model:"
25	
26	DEFAULT_MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-6"
27	
28	_ALIASES = {
29	    "sonnet": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
30	    "opus": "claude-opus-4-7",
31	    "haiku": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
32	}
33	
34	
35	def resolve(issue: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
36	    """Return the model the worker for ``issue`` should run on."""
37	    values = [
38	        label.removeprefix(_MODEL_LABEL_PREFIX)
39	        for label in issue.get("labels", [])
40	        if label.startswith(_MODEL_LABEL_PREFIX)
41	    ]
42	    if len(values) != 1:
43	        return DEFAULT_MODEL
44	    requested = values[0]
45	    return _ALIASES.get(requested, requested)
46

"""Per-issue worker-model resolution.

Workers default to a cost-efficient model. A spawned ``claude -p`` session
inherits whatever model the operator has globally pinned, which can be the
most expensive one — so unattended cycles default to Sonnet here and let an
individual issue opt up (or down) via a ``model:<alias>`` Linear label,
mirroring the ``repo:<name>`` mechanism in ``repos.py``.

Resolution is deliberately lenient and never raises: a worker always has a
safe model to run, so any ambiguity falls back to the default rather than
halting an unattended cycle. The model actually used is recorded in the run
log, so a mis-labelled issue surfaces after the fact instead of stalling.

* No ``model:`` label → ``DEFAULT_MODEL``.
* Exactly one label → its value, mapped through ``_ALIASES`` if known,
  otherwise passed through verbatim (``claude --model`` validates it).
* More than one label → ``DEFAULT_MODEL`` (ambiguous intent; the cheap,
  safe default wins).
"""
from __future__ import annotations

from typing import Any

_MODEL_LABEL_PREFIX = "model:"

DEFAULT_MODEL = "claude-sonnet-4-6"

_ALIASES = {
    "sonnet": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
    "opus": "claude-opus-4-7",
    "haiku": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
}


def resolve(issue: dict[str, Any]) -> str:
    """Return the model the worker for ``issue`` should run on."""
    values = [
        label.removeprefix(_MODEL_LABEL_PREFIX)
        for label in issue.get("labels", [])
        if label.startswith(_MODEL_LABEL_PREFIX)
    ]
    if len(values) != 1:
        return DEFAULT_MODEL
    requested = values[0]
    return _ALIASES.get(requested, requested)

1	"""Self-grade a series of drain-cycle runs.
2	
3	Reads every ``~/.drain-cycle/runs/*.json`` produced by the orchestrator
4	and emits a human-readable health read:
5	
6	1. Per-cycle section: cycle_id, attempted count, integer completion %,
7	   and halted entries as
8	   ``<identifier>: (<final_linear_state>, <exit_code>)``.
9	2. Across-cycles section: trend label over the last ``_TREND_WINDOW``
10	   cycles + recurrent ``(final_linear_state, exit_code)`` tuples
11	   appearing in ≥2 of those cycles.
12	3. Verdict section: one of ``HEALTHY`` / ``WATCH`` / ``CONCERNING``
13	   against the most-recent cycle's completion %. Labels are general
14	   health bands — the operator interprets them against whatever kill /
15	   continuation rule they hold for the work being drained. No
16	   project-specific reminders are baked into the tool.
17	
18	Run logs are grouped by ``cycle_id`` because one cycle can produce
19	multiple files when ``drain-cycle`` is re-run against the same cycle
20	after a halt. Chronological ordering uses the earliest filename per
21	cycle.
22	"""
23	from __future__ import annotations
24	
25	import json
26	import sys
27	from collections import Counter
28	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
29	from pathlib import Path
30	from typing import Any
31	
32	from . import runlog
33	
34	_TREND_WINDOW = 3
35	"""Number of most-recent cycles considered by the trend + recurrent-tuple
36	analysis. Pinned here; not configurable by the CLI."""
37	
38	_HEALTHY_THRESHOLD = 80
39	_WATCH_THRESHOLD = 50
40	"""Verdict bands. Boundaries are inclusive on the lower edge:
41	≥80 → HEALTHY, 50–79 → WATCH, <50 → CONCERNING."""
42	
43	
44	def default_runs_dir() -> Path:
45	    return runlog.runs_dir()
46	
47	
48	@dataclass
49	class _Cycle:
50	    cycle_id: str
51	    entries: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
52	    earliest_filename: str = ""
53	
54	    @property
55	    def attempted(self) -> int:
56	        return len(self.entries)
57	
58	    @property
59	    def completion_percent(self) -> int:
60	        if not self.entries:
61	            return 0
62	        done = sum(1 for e in self.entries if e["final_linear_state"] == "Done")
63	        return round(done * 100 / self.attempted)
64	
65	    @property
66	    def halted(self) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
67	        return [e for e in self.entries if e["final_linear_state"] != "Done"]
68	
69	
70	def _collect_cycles(runs_dir: Path) -> list[_Cycle]:
71	    """Group run-log files by cycle_id, chronological by earliest filename.
72	
73	    A cycle can span multiple files (re-running drain-cycle on the same
74	    cycle writes a new file). Ties are broken by filename; in practice
75	    cycles never tie because each cycle has its own UUID, but the rule is
76	    held for determinism if two cycles ever did share a min-filename
77	    timestamp.
78	
79	    Malformed files — invalid JSON, missing ``cycle_id`` — are skipped
80	    with a stderr warning. The grade tool is the kill-condition gauge;
81	    one bad file should never block reading the rest.
82	    """
83	    files = sorted(runs_dir.glob("*.json"))
84	    cycles: dict[str, _Cycle] = {}
85	    for path in files:
86	        try:
87	            payload = json.loads(path.read_text())
88	            cycle_id = payload["cycle_id"]
89	        except (json.JSONDecodeError, KeyError, OSError) as exc:
90	            print(
91	                f"drain-cycle grade: skipping malformed run-log {path}: {exc}",
92	                file=sys.stderr,
93	            )
94	            continue
95	        cycle = cycles.setdefault(cycle_id, _Cycle(cycle_id=cycle_id))
96	        cycle.entries.extend(payload.get("entries", []))
97	        if not cycle.earliest_filename or path.name < cycle.earliest_filename:
98	            cycle.earliest_filename = path.name
99	    return sorted(cycles.values(), key=lambda c: (c.earliest_filename, c.cycle_id))
100	
101	
102	def _trend_label(percents: list[int]) -> str:
103	    """Strict-monotonic over the window → improving/regressing; else flat.
104	
105	    Single-cycle or empty windows are "flat" by definition (no direction
106	    can be inferred from one or zero points). Two-cycle windows still
107	    apply the strict-monotonic rule (50 → 60 is improving; 50 → 50 is
108	    flat because the inequality is strict).
109	    """
110	    if len(percents) < 2:
111	        return "flat"
112	    if all(a < b for a, b in zip(percents, percents[1:])):
113	        return "improving"
114	    if all(a > b for a, b in zip(percents, percents[1:])):
115	        return "regressing"
116	    return "flat"
117	
118	
119	def _recurrent_tuples(cycles: list[_Cycle]) -> list[tuple[tuple[str, int], int]]:
120	    """Tuples appearing in ≥2 of the supplied cycles, with cycle-count.
121	
122	    Counted per cycle, not per entry: if a tuple appears three times
123	    inside one cycle and once in another, it counts as 2 cycles (not 4).
124	    Sorted by descending count then by the tuple itself for determinism.
125	    """
126	    counter: Counter[tuple[str, int]] = Counter()
127	    for cycle in cycles:
128	        seen_in_cycle: set[tuple[str, int]] = set()
129	        for entry in cycle.entries:
130	            key = (entry["final_linear_state"], entry["exit_code"])
131	            seen_in_cycle.add(key)
132	        counter.update(seen_in_cycle)
133	    recurrent = [(tup, count) for tup, count in counter.items() if count >= 2]
134	    recurrent.sort(key=lambda item: (-item[1], item[0]))
135	    return recurrent
136	
137	
138	def _render_across_cycles(cycles: list[_Cycle]) -> str:
139	    window = cycles[-_TREND_WINDOW:]
140	    percents = [c.completion_percent for c in window]
141	    lines = [
142	        f"window: last {len(window)} of {len(cycles)} cycle(s)"
143	        f" (max {_TREND_WINDOW})",
144	        f"trend: {_trend_label(percents)}",
145	    ]
146	    recurrent = _recurrent_tuples(window)
147	    if not recurrent:
148	        lines.append("recurrent tuples: none")
149	    else:
150	        lines.append("recurrent tuples:")
151	        for (state, exit_code), count in recurrent:
152	            lines.append(f"  ({state}, {exit_code}) x {count}")
153	    return "\n".join(lines)
154	
155	
156	def _render_verdict(cycles: list[_Cycle]) -> str:
157	    most_recent = cycles[-1]
158	    pct = most_recent.completion_percent
159	    if pct >= _HEALTHY_THRESHOLD:
160	        label = "HEALTHY"
161	    elif pct >= _WATCH_THRESHOLD:
162	        label = "WATCH"
163	    else:
164	        label = "CONCERNING"
165	    return f"verdict: {label} (most-recent cycle completion: {pct}%)"
166	
167	
168	def _render_per_cycle(cycle: _Cycle) -> str:
169	    lines = [
170	        f"cycle_id: {cycle.cycle_id}",
171	        f"  attempted: {cycle.attempted}",
172	        f"  completion: {cycle.completion_percent}%",
173	    ]
174	    if cycle.halted:
175	        lines.append("  halted:")
176	        for entry in cycle.halted:
177	            lines.append(
178	                f"    {entry['issue_identifier']}: "
179	                f"({entry['final_linear_state']}, {entry['exit_code']})"
180	            )
181	    return "\n".join(lines)
182	
183	
184	def run(runs_dir: Path) -> int:
185	    cycles = _collect_cycles(runs_dir) if runs_dir.is_dir() else []
186	    if not cycles:
187	        print(
188	            f"drain-cycle grade: no run logs found at {runs_dir}",
189	            file=sys.stderr,
190	        )
191	        return 1
192	
193	    print("== Per-cycle ==")
194	    print()
195	    for cycle in cycles:
196	        print(_render_per_cycle(cycle))
197	        print()
198	
199	    print("== Across cycles ==")
200	    print()
201	    print(_render_across_cycles(cycles))
202	    print()
203	
204	    print("== Verdict ==")
205	    print()
206	    print(_render_verdict(cycles))
207	    print()
208	    return 0
209
