aiTitle: Set up OpenTelemetry instrumentation with Honeycomb

input.old_string: - *Ignore blocks entirely.* Rejected: running a blocked issue wastes a worker on work that can't succeed by definition.
input.new_string:
- *Ignore blocks entirely.* Rejected: running a blocked issue wastes a worker on work that can't succeed by definition.

## 13. Opt-in OpenTelemetry tracing to Honeycomb

A drain runs unattended and can take hours, spending real tokens across many spawned sessions. The run log records the outcome per issue, but it is one flat file per invocation — it can't show where time went inside a drain, how the Linear round-trips and worker sessions nest, or let an operator aggregate cost across drains. Tracing fills that gap.

**Decision.** Each invocation emits one trace: a `drain.cycle` root span, a `drain.issue` span per attempted issue, and under those the `drain.worker.session`, `drain.worktree.add`/`.remove`, and per-operation `linear.*` spans. The `httpx` transport is auto-instrumented, so every Linear GraphQL POST appears as a child of its `linear.*` span. Worker token/cost/turn usage, the issue's repo/model/final-state, and the cycle outcome ride as span attributes; every halt site is tagged with a static `exception.slug` (greppable, low-cardinality, safe to `GROUP BY`). `service.name` is `drain-cycle`, which is also the Honeycomb dataset.

**Opt-in via `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY`.** The key's presence in the environment is the on/off switch. Absent, `telemetry.setup()` is a no-op and the default no-op tracer stays installed — a drain with no telemetry configured behaves exactly as before, takes no new network dependency at runtime, and the `start_as_current_span` calls scattered through the code cost nothing. The OTel packages are unconditional install-time dependencies (lightweight, pure-Python); only *exporting* is gated.

**Flush-on-exit is load-bearing.** `drain-cycle` is a short-lived CLI that exits through `sys.exit`. A `BatchSpanProcessor` buffers spans and exports on a timer, so without an explicit flush the queued spans die with the interpreter and the last issues of a drain never ship. `setup()` registers `shutdown()` (which flushes the processor) with `atexit`; `SystemExit` still runs `atexit` handlers, so every exit path drains the queue.

**Alternatives considered.**

- *`opentelemetry-instrument` zero-code agent.* Rejected: it wraps a `python` invocation, but the tool ships as a `uv tool` console-script entry point (`drain-cycle`), so there is no `python app.py` to wrap. Programmatic setup in `telemetry.setup()` is reliable regardless of how the entry point is launched.
- *Always-on tracing.* Rejected: it would force an exporter and an egress dependency on an operator who hasn't asked for it, and fail noisily (or silently retry) when Honeycomb is unreachable. Opt-in keeps the default path dependency-free.
- *Metrics and logs alongside traces.* Out of scope. The run log already covers durable per-issue accounting; traces add the causal/nesting view. A metrics layer can be added later if cost-rate alerting is wanted (see the otel-instrumentation layering guidance).
- *A span per private helper (e.g. `_plan`, `link_project_config`).* Rejected as over-instrumentation: those are fast, pure, and not independently aggregable. Interesting, failure-prone, or aggregable operations get spans; the rest stay as attributes on their parent.

+**Decision.** Each invocation emits one trace: a `drain.cycle` root span, a `drain.issue` span per attempted issue, and under those the `drain.worker.session`, `drain.worktree.add`/`.remove`, and per-operation `linear.*` spans. The `httpx` transport is auto-instrumented, so every Linear GraphQL POST appears as a child of its `linear.*` span. Worker token/cost/turn usage, the issue's repo/model/final-state, and the cycle outcome ride as span attributes; every halt site is tagged with a static `exception.slug` (greppable, low-cardinality, safe to `GROUP BY`). `service.name` is `drain-cycle`, which is also the Honeycomb dataset.

+**Opt-in via `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY`.** The key's presence in the environment is the on/off switch. Absent, `telemetry.setup()` is a no-op and the default no-op tracer stays installed — a drain with no telemetry configured behaves exactly as before, takes no new network dependency at runtime, and the `start_as_current_span` calls scattered through the code cost nothing. The OTel packages are unconditional install-time dependencies (lightweight, pure-Python); only *exporting* is gated.

+**Flush-on-exit is load-bearing.** `drain-cycle` is a short-lived CLI that exits through `sys.exit`. A `BatchSpanProcessor` buffers spans and exports on a timer, so without an explicit flush the queued spans die with the interpreter and the last issues of a drain never ship. `setup()` registers `shutdown()` (which flushes the processor) with `atexit`; `SystemExit` still runs `atexit` handlers, so every exit path drains the queue.

+- *`opentelemetry-instrument` zero-code agent.* Rejected: it wraps a `python` invocation, but the tool ships as a `uv tool` console-script entry point (`drain-cycle`), so there is no `python app.py` to wrap. Programmatic setup in `telemetry.setup()` is reliable regardless of how the entry point is launched.

+- *Always-on tracing.* Rejected: it would force an exporter and an egress dependency on an operator who hasn't asked for it, and fail noisily (or silently retry) when Honeycomb is unreachable. Opt-in keeps the default path dependency-free.

+- *Metrics and logs alongside traces.* Out of scope. The run log already covers durable per-issue accounting; traces add the causal/nesting view. A metrics layer can be added later if cost-rate alerting is wanted (see the otel-instrumentation layering guidance).

+- *A span per private helper (e.g. `_plan`, `link_project_config`).* Rejected as over-instrumentation: those are fast, pure, and not independently aggregable. Interesting, failure-prone, or aggregable operations get spans; the rest stay as attributes on their parent.
- *Best-effort run instead of defer/halt.* Rejected: silently violating a dependency the operator encoded is worse than a loud skip or stop.
- *Record deferrals in the run log.* Rejected: a deferred issue was never attempted; counting it as an entry corrupts `done/attempted` in `grade`.

## Design

input.new_string:
## Telemetry (optional)

drain-cycle can emit OpenTelemetry traces to [Honeycomb](https://www.honeycomb.io/) so you can see where a drain spends its time and tokens. It is **off unless `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY` is set** — with no key, tracing is a no-op and the tool takes no runtime network dependency.

```bash
echo 'HONEYCOMB_API_KEY=hcaik_…' >> ~/.drain-cycle/.env
```

Each invocation then emits one trace: a `drain.cycle` root span, a `drain.issue` span per attempted issue, and under those the spawned-session (`drain.worker.session`, carrying token/cost/turn usage), worktree (`drain.worktree.add`/`.remove`), and Linear (`linear.*`, wrapping the auto-instrumented `httpx` calls) spans. Traces land in a Honeycomb dataset named `drain-cycle`. Optional overrides: `HONEYCOMB_API_ENDPOINT` (default `https://api.honeycomb.io`; set the EU host for an EU team) and `OTEL_SERVICE_NAME` (default `drain-cycle`, which is also the dataset). See [`docs/design-decisions.md`](docs/design-decisions.md) §13.

## Design

Design decisions, alternatives considered, and deliberate out-of-scope choices live in [`docs/design-decisions.md`](docs/design-decisions.md).

input.prompt:
Review the uncommitted working-tree changes in the `drain-cycle` repo (`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle`). The change adds opt-in OpenTelemetry tracing to Honeycomb.

Run `git diff` for modified files and read the new untracked file `drain_cycle/telemetry.py` in full. Files in scope:
- `drain_cycle/telemetry.py` (NEW) — SDK setup, Honeycomb OTLP exporter, httpx auto-instrumentation, atexit flush, `mark_error` helper
- `drain_cycle/cli.py` — calls `telemetry.setup()` after secrets load
- `drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` — wraps drain in a `drain.cycle` span; **extracted** the per-issue loop body into a new `_drain_one_issue` helper that opens a `drain.issue` span. The behavior must be IDENTICAL to before the refactor — verify the extraction preserved all halt paths, the cycle-breach check placement, the Done `continue`→`return None` translation, and run-log/Linear-state semantics.
- `drain_cycle/worker.py` — wraps `run_issue` body in a `drain.worker.session` span + `_annotate_worker_span`
- `drain_cycle/worktree.py` — spans around add/remove
- `drain_cycle/linear.py` — span per GraphQL op via new `operation` kwarg on `_post`; exception slugs
- test + doc + pyproject/uv.lock changes

Focus your review on:
1. **Correctness of the orchestrator extraction** — this is the highest-risk change. Confirm no behavior drift versus the original single-function `run`. Read `git diff drain_cycle/orchestrator.py` carefully.
2. **Flush-on-exit correctness** — the CLI exits via `sys.exit`; confirm spans actually flush (atexit + BatchSpanProcessor.shutdown), and that `shutdown()` is idempotent (registered atexit + could be called explicitly).
3. **No-op-when-disabled** — when `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY` is unset, confirm there is zero behavior change and no network dependency (the module-level `tracer` must be safe before/without `setup()`).
4. **Thread-safety / contextvars** — `worker.py` uses a reader thread; confirm span context propagation is sound and the worker span isn't created/ended across threads incorrectly.
5. Standard axes: readability, error handling, security (the Honeycomb key handling), and adherence to the repo's comment rules in AGENTS.md (no ticket IDs / fix-history narration in code comments).

Do NOT make changes — report findings grouped by severity (Critical / Required / Optional / Nit) with file:line references. Run `uv run pytest -q` and report the result. Be concise.

content.text:
All five `_post` call sites pass `operation` as keyword. The `HTTPXClientInstrumentor().instrument()` is global/process-wide (instruments the httpx module), only invoked inside `setup()` so it's a no-op when disabled. 

Now I have everything I need. Let me check one final security detail: whether the Honeycomb API key could leak into span attributes or logs. The key goes only into the OTLP exporter headers (`x-honeycomb-team`) and Linear key into the `Authorization` header — neither is set as a span attribute. Good. Let me also confirm `shutdown()` idempotency under the swap pattern is concurrency-safe enough for the atexit single-thread context.

I have all findings. Here is my review.

---

## Review Summary

**Verdict:** APPROVE (with one Required doc fix)

**Overview:** Opt-in OpenTelemetry/Honeycomb tracing added across the orchestrator, worker, worktree, and Linear client, plus a new `telemetry.py`. The high-risk orchestrator extraction is behavior-preserving, the no-op-when-disabled contract holds, flush-on-exit is correct, and the worker span has no cross-thread hazard. All 197 tests pass. One real documentation defect was introduced and should be fixed before merge.

### Critical Issues
None.

### Required Issues

- **`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/docs/design-decisions.md:224-225`** — Section 13 was inserted into the middle of section 12's "Alternatives considered" list, orphaning two bullets. In `HEAD`, section 12's alternatives were four bullets (lines 205-208): *Keep priority*, *Ignore blocks entirely*, *Best-effort run*, *Record deferrals*. The new `## 13` heading was placed after the second bullet, so *Best-effort run* and *Record deferrals* now sit under section 13's "Alternatives considered" where they make no sense (they are about defer/halt, not telemetry). Fix: move those two bullets back up to immediately after line 206 (the end of section 12's list), before the `## 13` heading. This is a Rule D6 / artefact-integrity issue — the doc is the durable artefact a future agent reads.

### Optional Issues

- **`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/telemetry.py` (no test)** — There is no test for the new module, and no other test file references `telemetry`. The two load-bearing invariants the PR description itself flags — (a) `setup()` returns `False` and installs nothing when `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY` is unset, and (b) `shutdown()` is idempotent across the explicit+atexit double-call — are exactly the kind of thing a 3-line unit test would pin. The orchestrator/worker/linear tests pass only because the no-op tracer is transparent, so they prove "telemetry doesn't break the happy path" but not "telemetry behaves as specified." Consider a `test_telemetry.py` asserting `setup()` is `False` with the key absent, that `_provider` stays `None`, and that a second `shutdown()` is a no-op (monkeypatch a fake provider to count `.shutdown()` calls).

- **`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/telemetry.py:86-92`** — `shutdown()` is idempotent via the `provider, _provider = _provider, None` swap, which is fine for the actual call sites (atexit + a possible explicit call, both single-threaded). It is not atomic under true concurrency, but nothing in this CLI calls it from multiple threads, so this is a note, not a defect. The docstring claim "Idempotent" is accurate for the real usage.

- **`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/telemetry.py:74-77`** — The OTLP exporter has no explicit `timeout`. If Honeycomb is unreachable, `BatchSpanProcessor.shutdown()` (called at exit) will block on the default exporter timeout (10s) before the CLI exits. Acceptable for an opt-in path the operator chose, but worth a one-line awareness note; the design doc's "fail noisily when Honeycomb is unreachable" framing covers the always-on rejection but not the at-exit flush latency on the opt-in path.

### Nits

- **`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/linear.py:60`** — On the no-API-key path you `mark_error(span, ...)` then `raise RuntimeError`. The span is correctly closed by the `with` block on the raised exception. Consistent and fine; just noting the `error=true` attribute will now be set on `linear.*` spans for the missing-key case even when telemetry happens to be on, which is the intended behavior.

- **PR-description claim verification** — the worker span (`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/worker.py:166-232`) is created, attributed, and ended entirely within `run_issue`'s own thread. The reader thread only invokes `on_progress` callbacks and never touches `span`, so there is no cross-thread span start/end. Context propagation into the spawned `claude` subprocess does not happen (no OTEL env injection), which is correct — that process is out of scope.

### What's Done Well

- The orchestrator extraction is genuinely behavior-preserving, which was the stated highest risk. I diffed the extracted `_drain_one_issue` against `HEAD`'s inline loop body line by line: every halt path (repo-resolution, setup-failed, per-issue breach, not-Done) returns `1`, the Done path's `continue` is faithfully translated to `return None`, and the cycle-wide circuit breaker now runs in the caller *after* `_drain_one_issue` returns `None` — which is exactly where the original `continue` placed it (after the Done append, before the next iteration). Run-log `append_entry` calls, revert semantics, and the `started_at`/`finished_at` timing are all preserved. `cycle_id` is correctly threaded through as a parameter where it was previously a closure variable.
- The no-op-when-disabled contract is real: `tracer` is bound at import via the OTel proxy provider, SDK imports are deferred inside `setup()`, and `HTTPXClientInstrumentor().instrument()` only runs when a key is present — so an unconfigured run takes no SDK code path and no network dependency, matching the docstring and design-decisions §13.
- Flush-on-exit reasoning is correct and well-documented: `atexit` handlers do run on `sys.exit`/`SystemExit`, and `shutdown()` → `provider.shutdown()` flushes the `BatchSpanProcessor`. The `_provider`-gate makes the atexit + explicit double-call flush exactly once.
- Security is clean: neither the Honeycomb key (`x-honeycomb-team` header) nor the Linear key (`Authorization` header) is ever written to a span attribute or log line. The `mark_error` slugs are deliberately static and low-cardinality per the comment, which is the right call for `GROUP BY` safety.
- Comment hygiene complies with AGENTS.md: no ticket IDs, no commit SHAs, no fix-history narration. Comments explain invariants as they stand (e.g. the prompt-positional ordering, the circuit-breaker rationale).

### Verification Story

- **Tests reviewed:** Yes. The only test changes are mechanical signature updates to `fake_post` adding the new keyword-only `operation` param — correct and necessary since `_post` gained `*, operation`. All five real `_post` call sites pass `operation` by keyword, so nothing breaks. No new telemetry-specific tests were added (see Optional).
- **Build verified:** Yes. `uv run pytest -q` → **197 passed in 17.97s**.
- **Security checked:** Yes. API keys stay in HTTP headers; no key material reaches span attributes or stderr. No new untrusted-input boundary introduced.

Acceptable for an opt-in path the operator chose, but worth a one-line awareness note; the design doc's "fail noisily when Honeycomb is unreachable" framing covers the always-on rejection but not the at-exit flush latency on the opt-in path.

### Nits

- **`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/linear.py:60`** — On the no-API-key path you `mark_error(span, ...)` then `raise RuntimeError`. The span is correctly closed by the `with` block on the raised exception. Consistent and fine; just noting the `error=true` attribute will now be set on `linear.*` spans for the missing-key case even when telemetry happens to be on, which is the intended behavior.

- **PR-description claim verification** — the worker span (`/Users/anton/src/drain-cycle/drain_cycle/worker.py:166-232`) is created, attributed, and ended entirely within `run_issue`'s own thread. The reader thread only invokes `on_progress` callbacks and never touches `span`, so there is no cross-thread span start/end. Context propagation into the spawned `claude` subprocess does not happen (no OTEL env injection), which is correct — that process is out of scope.

### What's Done Well

- The orchestrator extraction is genuinely behavior-preserving, which was the stated highest risk. I diffed the extracted `_drain_one_issue` against `HEAD`'s inline loop body line by line: every halt path (repo-resolution, setup-failed, per-issue breach, not-Done) returns `1`, the Done path's `continue` is faithfully translated to `return None`, and the cycle-wide circuit breaker now runs in the caller *after* `_drain_one_issue` returns `None` — which is exactly where the original `continue` placed it (after the Done append, before the next iteration). Run-log `append_entry` calls, revert semantics, and the `started_at`/`finished_at` timing are all preserved. `cycle_id` is correctly threaded through as a parameter where it was previously a closure variable.
- The no-op-when-disabled contract is real: `tracer` is bound at import via the OTel proxy provider, SDK imports are deferred inside `setup()`, and `HTTPXClientInstrumentor().instrument()` only runs when a key is present — so an unconfigured run takes no SDK code path and no network dependency, matching the docstring and design-decisions §13.
- Flush-on-exit reasoning is correct and well-documented: `atexit` handlers do run on `sys.exit`/`SystemExit`, and `shutdown()` → `provider.shutdown()` flushes the `BatchSpanProcessor`. The `_provider`-gate makes the atexit + explicit double-call flush exactly once.
- Security is clean: neither the Honeycomb key (`x-honeycomb-team` header) nor the Linear key (`Authorization` header) is ever written to a span attribute or log line. The `mark_error` slugs are deliberately static and low-cardinality per the comment, which is the right call for `GROUP BY` safety.
- Comment hygiene complies with AGENTS.md: no ticket IDs, no commit SHAs, no fix-history narration. Comments explain invariants as they stand (e.g. the prompt-positional ordering, the circuit-breaker rationale).

### Verification Story

- **Tests reviewed:** Yes. The only test changes are mechanical signature updates to `fake_post` adding the new keyword-only `operation` param — correct and necessary since `_post` gained `*, operation`. All five real `_post` call sites pass `operation` by keyword, so nothing breaks. No new telemetry-specific tests were added (see Optional).
- **Build verified:** Yes. `uv run pytest -q` → **197 passed in 17.97s**.
- **Security checked:** Yes. API keys stay in HTTP headers; no key material reaches span attributes or stderr. No new untrusted-input boundary introduced.

The reviewer approved with one Required fix: my §13 insertion split §12's "Alternatives considered" list, orphaning two bullets. (My `tail` anchor misled me — two bullets followed it.) Let me read the boundary and fix.

content.content:
203	**Alternatives considered.**
204	
205	- *Keep priority in the sort key.* Rejected: the operator has one predictable knob — the manual drag-order — and priority overriding it is surprising and undesirable.
206	- *Ignore blocks entirely.* Rejected: running a blocked issue wastes a worker on work that can't succeed by definition.
207	
208	## 13. Opt-in OpenTelemetry tracing to Honeycomb
209	
210	A drain runs unattended and can take hours, spending real tokens across many spawned sessions. The run log records the outcome per issue, but it is one flat file per invocation — it can't show where time went inside a drain, how the Linear round-trips and worker sessions nest, or let an operator aggregate cost across drains. Tracing fills that gap.
211	
212	**Decision.** Each invocation emits one trace: a `drain.cycle` root span, a `drain.issue` span per attempted issue, and under those the `drain.worker.session`, `drain.worktree.add`/`.remove`, and per-operation `linear.*` spans. The `httpx` transport is auto-instrumented, so every Linear GraphQL POST appears as a child of its `linear.*` span. Worker token/cost/turn usage, the issue's repo/model/final-state, and the cycle outcome ride as span attributes; every halt site is tagged with a static `exception.slug` (greppable, low-cardinality, safe to `GROUP BY`). `service.name` is `drain-cycle`, which is also the Honeycomb dataset.
213	
214	**Opt-in via `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY`.** The key's presence in the environment is the on/off switch. Absent, `telemetry.setup()` is a no-op and the default no-op tracer stays installed — a drain with no telemetry configured behaves exactly as before, takes no new network dependency at runtime, and the `start_as_current_span` calls scattered through the code cost nothing. The OTel packages are unconditional install-time dependencies (lightweight, pure-Python); only *exporting* is gated.
215	
216	**Flush-on-exit is load-bearing.** `drain-cycle` is a short-lived CLI that exits through `sys.exit`. A `BatchSpanProcessor` buffers spans and exports on a timer, so without an explicit flush the queued spans die with the interpreter and the last issues of a drain never ship. `setup()` registers `shutdown()` (which flushes the processor) with `atexit`; `SystemExit` still runs `atexit` handlers, so every exit path drains the queue.
217	
218	**Alternatives considered.**
219	
220	- *`opentelemetry-instrument` zero-code agent.* Rejected: it wraps a `python` invocation, but the tool ships as a `uv tool` console-script entry point (`drain-cycle`), so there is no `python app.py` to wrap. Programmatic setup in `telemetry.setup()` is reliable regardless of how the entry point is launched.
221	- *Always-on tracing.* Rejected: it would force an exporter and an egress dependency on an operator who hasn't asked for it, and fail noisily (or silently retry) when Honeycomb is unreachable. Opt-in keeps the default path dependency-free.
222	- *Metrics and logs alongside traces.* Out of scope. The run log already covers durable per-issue accounting; traces add the causal/nesting view. A metrics layer can be added later if cost-rate alerting is wanted (see the otel-instrumentation layering guidance).
223	- *A span per private helper (e.g. `_plan`, `link_project_config`).* Rejected as over-instrumentation: those are fast, pure, and not independently aggregable. Interesting, failure-prone, or aggregable operations get spans; the rest stay as attributes on their parent.
224	- *Best-effort run instead of defer/halt.* Rejected: silently violating a dependency the operator encoded is worse than a loud skip or stop.
225	- *Record deferrals in the run log.* Rejected: a deferred issue was never attempted; counting it as an entry corrupts `done/attempted` in `grade`.
226

file.content:
**Alternatives considered.**

- *Keep priority in the sort key.* Rejected: the operator has one predictable knob — the manual drag-order — and priority overriding it is surprising and undesirable.
- *Ignore blocks entirely.* Rejected: running a blocked issue wastes a worker on work that can't succeed by definition.

## 13. Opt-in OpenTelemetry tracing to Honeycomb

A drain runs unattended and can take hours, spending real tokens across many spawned sessions. The run log records the outcome per issue, but it is one flat file per invocation — it can't show where time went inside a drain, how the Linear round-trips and worker sessions nest, or let an operator aggregate cost across drains. Tracing fills that gap.

**Decision.** Each invocation emits one trace: a `drain.cycle` root span, a `drain.issue` span per attempted issue, and under those the `drain.worker.session`, `drain.worktree.add`/`.remove`, and per-operation `linear.*` spans. The `httpx` transport is auto-instrumented, so every Linear GraphQL POST appears as a child of its `linear.*` span. Worker token/cost/turn usage, the issue's repo/model/final-state, and the cycle outcome ride as span attributes; every halt site is tagged with a static `exception.slug` (greppable, low-cardinality, safe to `GROUP BY`). `service.name` is `drain-cycle`, which is also the Honeycomb dataset.

**Opt-in via `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY`.** The key's presence in the environment is the on/off switch. Absent, `telemetry.setup()` is a no-op and the default no-op tracer stays installed — a drain with no telemetry configured behaves exactly as before, takes no new network dependency at runtime, and the `start_as_current_span` calls scattered through the code cost nothing. The OTel packages are unconditional install-time dependencies (lightweight, pure-Python); only *exporting* is gated.

**Flush-on-exit is load-bearing.** `drain-cycle` is a short-lived CLI that exits through `sys.exit`. A `BatchSpanProcessor` buffers spans and exports on a timer, so without an explicit flush the queued spans die with the interpreter and the last issues of a drain never ship. `setup()` registers `shutdown()` (which flushes the processor) with `atexit`; `SystemExit` still runs `atexit` handlers, so every exit path drains the queue.

**Alternatives considered.**

- *`opentelemetry-instrument` zero-code agent.* Rejected: it wraps a `python` invocation, but the tool ships as a `uv tool` console-script entry point (`drain-cycle`), so there is no `python app.py` to wrap. Programmatic setup in `telemetry.setup()` is reliable regardless of how the entry point is launched.
- *Always-on tracing.* Rejected: it would force an exporter and an egress dependency on an operator who hasn't asked for it, and fail noisily (or silently retry) when Honeycomb is unreachable. Opt-in keeps the default path dependency-free.
- *Metrics and logs alongside traces.* Out of scope. The run log already covers durable per-issue accounting; traces add the causal/nesting view. A metrics layer can be added later if cost-rate alerting is wanted (see the otel-instrumentation layering guidance).
- *A span per private helper (e.g. `_plan`, `link_project_config`).* Rejected as over-instrumentation: those are fast, pure, and not independently aggregable. Interesting, failure-prone, or aggregable operations get spans; the rest stay as attributes on their parent.
- *Best-effort run instead of defer/halt.* Rejected: silently violating a dependency the operator encoded is worse than a loud skip or stop.
- *Record deferrals in the run log.* Rejected: a deferred issue was never attempted; counting it as an entry corrupts `done/attempted` in `grade`.

content.text: Doc fixed. The reviewer also flagged (Optional) that the two load-bearing telemetry invariants — no-op when the key is absent, and idempotent shutdown — aren't pinned by a test. That's a cheap, worthwhile addition, so I'll add a focused `test_telemetry.py`.

input.content:
"""Unit tests for the telemetry module's load-bearing invariants.

These pin the two behaviours the rest of the suite relies on but can't observe
(it runs with no ``HONEYCOMB_API_KEY``, so the no-op tracer is transparent):
``setup`` does nothing without a key, and ``shutdown`` flushes exactly once.
``setup`` is never called *with* a key here — that would install a global
provider and instrument ``httpx`` process-wide, leaking into other tests.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import pytest
from opentelemetry.trace import StatusCode

from drain_cycle import telemetry


def test_setup_is_noop_without_api_key(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    monkeypatch.delenv("HONEYCOMB_API_KEY", raising=False)
    monkeypatch.setattr(telemetry, "_provider", None)

    assert telemetry.setup() is False
    assert telemetry._provider is None


def test_shutdown_flushes_once_and_is_idempotent(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    class FakeProvider:
        def __init__(self) -> None:
            self.shutdowns = 0

        def shutdown(self) -> None:
            self.shutdowns += 1

    fake = FakeProvider()
    monkeypatch.setattr(telemetry, "_provider", fake)

    telemetry.shutdown()
    telemetry.shutdown()

    assert fake.shutdowns == 1
    assert telemetry._provider is None


def test_shutdown_without_setup_is_safe(monkeypatch: pytest.MonkeyPatch) -> None:
    monkeypatch.setattr(telemetry, "_provider", None)
    telemetry.shutdown()  # must not raise


def test_mark_error_sets_flag_slug_and_error_status() -> None:
    class FakeSpan:
        def __init__(self) -> None:
            self.attrs: dict[str, object] = {}
            self.status = None

        def set_attribute(self, key: str, value: object) -> None:
            self.attrs[key] = value

        def set_status(self, status: object) -> None:
            self.status = status

    span = FakeSpan()
    telemetry.mark_error(span, "err-test-slug", "boom")

    assert span.attrs["error"] is True
    assert span.attrs["exception.slug"] == "err-test-slug"
    assert span.status is not None
    assert span.status.status_code == StatusCode.ERROR

content.text:
All 201 pass (197 + 4 new telemetry tests). Done — here's the complete picture.

## What was built

Opt-in OpenTelemetry → Honeycomb tracing across `drain-cycle`, gated entirely on `HONEYCOMB_API_KEY` (absent → no-op, zero behavior change, no runtime network dependency):

- **`telemetry.py`** (new) — SDK setup, Honeycomb OTLP/HTTP exporter, **httpx auto-instrumentation**, `atexit` flush-on-exit (load-bearing for a `sys.exit`-based CLI), and a `mark_error` helper for greppable `exception.slug`s.
- **Custom spans** — `drain.cycle` (root) → `drain.issue` → `drain.worker.session` / `drain.worktree.add|remove` / `linear.*`, with the httpx POST auto-instrumented under each `linear.*` span. Worker token/cost/turn usage, issue repo/model/state, and halt slugs ride as attributes.
- Wired `telemetry.setup()` into `cli.main`; the orchestrator's per-issue loop was extracted to `_drain_one_issue` (behavior-preserving) to host the issue span.

## Verification (real data in Honeycomb, dataset `drain-cycle`, env `test`)

A controlled smoke run — **real** read-only Linear calls + a representative drain trace, no worker spawned, no Linear mutation — confirmed end-to-end:

- **Auto-instrumentation**: 2× `POST` (httpx) spans nested under `linear.current_cycle` / `linear.pending_issues` (real API, `duration_ms=510.98`).
- **Custom spans + attributes**: `drain.worker.session` carried `worker.cost_usd=2.47`, `worker.tokens.cumulative=1234567`, `worker.num_turns=42`; resource `service.name=drain-cycle`, `service.version=0.1.0`, SDK `opentelemetry/python/1.42.1`.
- **Error path**: `drain.issue` showed `error=true`, `exception.slug=err-per-issue-breach`, ERROR status + message; correct `issue.identifier/exit_code/is_done/model/repo`.

## Review

Senior code-reviewer sub-agent: **APPROVE**. One Required finding (my §13 doc insertion had orphaned two of §12's bullets) — **fixed**. I also took its Optional suggestion and added `test_telemetry.py` pinning the no-op-when-disabled and idempotent-shutdown invariants. Security clean: neither the Honeycomb nor Linear key ever reaches a span attribute or log.

## Two things for you

1. **Not committed yet.** Per the base "commit/push only when asked" rule, I've held off — even though this repo's convention is to push straight to `main`. Want me to commit and push? (Diff: `telemetry.py` + 5 module edits + tests + README/design-decisions §13 + pyproject/uv.lock.)
2. **Rotate the key.** You pasted `HtK9rhzSRFXZCEo6M00WDD` in chat; it's stored only in `~/.drain-cycle/.env` (gitignored location, not in the repo), but since it's now in this transcript, consider regenerating it in Honeycomb.

## honeycomb
Honeycomb MCP Server - Observability Data Query Interface

<role>
You are an observability assistant for Honeycomb users. You investigate production behavior — performance, errors, deployment impact, alert state — by querying real telemetry through these tools. The workspace's actual state is only knowable through tool calls; do not reason from priors about what services, datasets, or alerts exist.

When the user names a dataset, span, or service, treat it as a starting point for discovery, not a license to skip it. The right span name within a dataset is rarely obvious from the dataset name alone, and aggregating the wrong attribute or the wrong span is the most common cause of misleading answers.
</role>

<workflow>
For most observability questions — including "how many", "what's slow", "what's erroring" — start with span discovery (list_spans, get_span_details). These tools return counts and attribute distributions directly; they often answer the question without a follow-up query. Reach for run_query only when you need a calculation, distribution, or comparison those tools can't express. Span tools work across all trace-aware datasets in scope without needing to know which dataset to target.

1. ORIENT
   - Call 'get_workspace_context' to learn the team, environments, and dataset counts. Use the returned environment slug in subsequent calls.

2. DISCOVER (pick the path that matches the question)
   - Span discovery — for "what's happening / what's slow / how many X / what does service X do":
     - 'list_spans' ranks span names by count with root_count to distinguish entry points from internal helpers. Counts here are often the answer to "how many X happened".
     - 'get_span_details' on a chosen span_name returns its populated attributes, value cardinality, and top observed values — including which spans actually populate a count-bearing attribute.
     Answer directly from these when you can — composing an equivalent run_query by hand is slower and more error-prone.
   - Alert / SLO state — for "what's bro… [truncated]

aiTitle: Set up OpenTelemetry instrumentation with Honeycomb

content.text: The user invoked `/commit`. I'll stage everything and create one commit.

input.command:
git add -A && git commit -q -F - <<'EOF'
feat: opt-in OpenTelemetry tracing to Honeycomb

Emit one trace per drain: a drain.cycle root span, a drain.issue span per
attempted issue, and under those the spawned-session, worktree, and Linear
spans. httpx is auto-instrumented, so each Linear GraphQL POST nests under
its linear.<operation> span. Worker token/cost/turn usage and issue
repo/model/state ride as attributes; halt sites carry a static
exception.slug.

Gated on HONEYCOMB_API_KEY: absent, setup() is a no-op and the default
no-op tracer stays installed, so an unconfigured drain is unchanged and
takes no runtime network dependency. setup() registers an atexit flush so
the BatchSpanProcessor drains before the short-lived CLI exits.

The orchestrator's per-issue loop body is extracted into _drain_one_issue
(behavior-preserving) to host the drain.issue span; the cycle-wide circuit
breaker stays in the caller, where the original continue placed it.
